title: AI Systems
text: Updated: Jun 2026

title: Preface and Overview
text:
Why This Guide Exists
AI is now part of everyday learning, research, analysis, writing, coding, decision-making, and professional work.
But using AI is not the same as building an AI system.
A person can ask a chatbot a question and get an answer.
That is AI use.
A person can design a workflow where the problem is clearly framed, the context is provided, the output is evaluated, the limitations are documented, and the final decision remains human-led.
That is an AI system.
This guide is about the second path.
It teaches AI as a practical system for responsible, reproducible, and impactful work.
Many people learn AI through tools.
They learn prompts.
They learn apps.
They learn shortcuts.
That is useful, but incomplete.
Tools change quickly. Workflows, reasoning habits, evaluation practices, and documentation standards last longer.
The purpose of this guide is to help learners move beyond casual AI use into structured AI practice.
The goal is not to make AI mysterious.
The goal is to make AI usable, inspectable, teachable, and accountable.
In Complex Data Insights, AI is treated as part of a broader data and decision ecosystem.
AI can support discovery, analysis, communication, automation, and learning. But it must remain connected to human judgment, evidence, context, and responsibility.

title: Preface and Overview
text:
The Core Philosophy
The central philosophy of this guide is simple:

AI should assist human thinking, not replace human responsibility.

This guide uses a Human–AI–Human model.
Human frames the problem
        ↓
AI assists with generation, reasoning, analysis, or automation
        ↓
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts
The workflow begins with a human question and ends with a human decision.
AI sits in the middle as an assistant, accelerator, analyst, reviewer, coder, summarizer, or workflow component.
This framing is important because AI outputs can be fluent without being correct. They can be useful without being complete. They can be impressive without being reliable.
A responsible AI system must therefore include evaluation, review, and documentation from the beginning.

title: Preface and Overview
text:
Relationship to Thinking with AI
This guide connects naturally to the earlier Thinking with AI idea, but it is not the same thing.
Thinking with AI focuses on reasoning habits:

asking better questions,
exploring ideas,
challenging assumptions,
improving explanations,
reflecting before deciding,
and using AI to strengthen human thinking.

AI Systems is broader and more operational.
It asks how AI-supported work can be designed, evaluated, repeated, documented, governed, and shared.
The relationship can be viewed as:
Thinking with AI
        ↓
Human–AI–Human reasoning loop
        ↓
AI Systems
        ↓
Applied workflows, evaluation, governance, and impact
Thinking with AI is therefore a foundation inside AI Systems.
AI Systems extends that foundation into real workflows.

title: Preface and Overview
text:
Who This Guide Is For
This guide is for people who want to use AI seriously and responsibly.
It is especially useful for:

students learning how to work with AI without becoming dependent on it,
researchers using AI to support reading, synthesis, coding, analysis, or writing,
data scientists building reproducible AI-assisted workflows,
educators designing AI-aware learning activities,
professionals using AI to improve productivity and communication,
mentors training learners for an AI-enabled workplace,
and teams that need governance, documentation, and quality control.

The guide does not assume that the reader is an AI engineer.
It does assume that the reader wants to think carefully, work reproducibly, and communicate limitations honestly.

title: Preface and Overview
text:
What This Guide Is Not
This guide is not a collection of prompt tricks.
It is not a list of AI tools.
It is not a promise that AI can solve every problem.
It is not a replacement for domain expertise, statistical reasoning, data science, ethics, or human judgment.
Instead, it is a system-level guide for using AI in a structured way.
The focus is on workflows that can be explained, repeated, evaluated, improved, and responsibly shared.

title: Preface and Overview
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of this guide, the learner should be able to:

Explain AI as part of a broader human-led system.
Distinguish casual AI use from structured AI workflows.
Frame AI tasks with clear goals, context, constraints, and expected outputs.
Design prompts and context packages that improve output quality.
Use grounding materials such as data, documents, code, examples, and references.
Evaluate AI outputs for correctness, relevance, completeness, clarity, and risk.
Build human-in-the-loop review workflows.
Document AI use transparently.
Identify governance, privacy, bias, and safety concerns.
Create reproducible AI-assisted workflows that support real work.
Communicate AI-supported outputs without overstating certainty.
Prepare learners and professionals for responsible AI-enabled careers.

title: Preface and Overview
text:
The AI Systems Architecture
This guide organizes AI work into a layered system.
Problem
  ↓
Context
  ↓
Data and Knowledge
  ↓
Prompt or Workflow Design
  ↓
AI Assistance
  ↓
Evaluation
  ↓
Human Decision
  ↓
Documentation and Impact
Each layer matters.
A weak problem statement produces unclear outputs.
Poor context produces shallow responses.
Ungrounded AI work increases the risk of error.
No evaluation makes outputs difficult to trust.
No documentation makes the workflow difficult to repeat or defend.
A strong AI system connects all of these layers.

title: Preface and Overview
text:
Guide Structure
The guide is organized into four major sections.

Foundation
The foundation chapters introduce AI systems thinking, AI literacy, responsible use, and problem framing.
These chapters establish the mindset needed before building applied workflows.


Applied AI Workflows
The applied workflow chapters focus on prompt design, context design, grounding, evaluation, and human review.
These chapters move from general AI awareness to reproducible AI practice.


Operational AI Systems
The operational chapters focus on automation, agentic workflows, AI-assisted analysis, reporting, governance, documentation, and an end-to-end case study.
These chapters show how AI can become part of real systems without removing human responsibility.


Growth and Reference
The final section connects AI systems to career readiness, workforce development, appendices, and references.
This section supports learners who want to use AI as part of long-term professional growth.

text:
Supporting Scripts
The scaffold includes small scripts to demonstrate AI systems practice without requiring live API keys.
The first example workflow creates a simple AI task log, evaluates the quality of AI-supported outputs, builds a prompt registry, and plots an evaluation summary.
bash scripts/bash/00-check-environment.sh
bash scripts/bash/01-create-example-ai-task-log.sh

python scripts/python/02-evaluate-ai-output-quality.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results

python scripts/python/03-build-prompt-registry.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results

Rscript scripts/R/04-plot-evaluation-summary.R \
  data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv \
  data/reports
Expected outputs include:
data/example/ai-task-log.csv
data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv
data/results/prompt-registry.csv
data/reports/ai-evaluation-summary.png
These examples are intentionally lightweight.
They teach the structure of responsible AI workflows without forcing learners to use paid APIs or expose secret keys.

title: Preface and Overview
text:
How to Read This Guide
This guide can be read sequentially, but it is also designed as a reference.
Readers who are new to AI should begin with the foundation chapters.
Readers who already use AI regularly may want to focus on evaluation, human review, governance, and documentation.
Teams building workflows may want to start with the end-to-end case study and then return to earlier chapters as needed.
The most important idea is repeated throughout the guide:

AI work becomes stronger when it is framed, grounded, evaluated, documented, and kept human-led.

title: Preface and Overview
text:
Closing Note
AI systems are not only technical systems.
They are learning systems.
They are communication systems.
They are decision-support systems.
They are professional systems.
They affect how people think, work, teach, analyze, and act.
For that reason, AI should be approached with both curiosity and discipline.
This guide begins from that balance.

title: Preface and Overview
text:
Looking Ahead

From there, we move step by step from AI literacy into problem framing, prompt and context design, data and knowledge grounding, evaluation, human review, automation, governance, reporting, and workforce readiness.
By the end of this guide, you should be able to move beyond isolated AI use and begin designing responsible, reproducible, human-led AI workflows.
The central pattern throughout the guide is the Human–AI–Human workflow:
Human defines the problem.
AI assists with generation, reasoning, analysis, or automation.
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts.

title: AI Systems Thinking
text:
Why AI Systems Thinking Matters
AI is often introduced as a tool.
A chatbot.
A writing assistant.
A coding helper.
A search companion.
A summarizer.
Those uses are helpful, but they are only the beginning.
The real value of AI appears when it is used as part of a system.
A system has a purpose.
It has inputs.
It has a workflow.
It has quality checks.
It has human judgment.
It has outputs that can be reviewed, improved, reused, and trusted.
This chapter introduces AI systems thinking: the habit of designing AI use around problems, people, evidence, decisions, and accountability rather than around isolated prompts.
Many people first experience AI as a conversation.
They ask a question.
The model responds.
They accept, reject, or revise the answer.
That is useful, but it can also create a weak habit: treating AI output as the final product.
AI systems thinking changes the question from:
into:
This shift matters because real work usually requires more than generation.
Real work requires clarity, context, evaluation, documentation, and responsibility.
For example, a learner may ask AI to explain a concept.
An analyst may ask AI to summarize results.
A researcher may ask AI to help structure a report.
A professional may ask AI to draft a decision memo.
In each case, the AI output is not enough by itself.
The human still needs to frame the task, check the output, compare it with evidence, decide what to use, and explain the final result.
That is why CDI treats AI as part of a human-led system.

title: AI Systems Thinking
text:
What can AI generate?



What workflow are we building, and where should AI assist?

title: AI Systems Thinking
section: From AI Tool Use to AI System Design
text:
From AI Tool Use to AI System Design
Tool use asks:
Can this AI help me complete this task?
System design asks:
How should this task be structured so AI support is useful, safe, repeatable, and reviewable?
The difference is important.
A tool-use mindset often leads to one-off outputs.
A systems mindset creates reusable workflows.



Tool-use mindset
Systems mindset




Ask a prompt
Define the problem


Get an answer
Design a workflow


Copy useful text
Review and improve output


Trust based on fluency
Trust based on checks


Start over each time
Reuse templates and patterns


Focus on speed
Balance speed, quality, and responsibility



AI systems thinking does not reject simple AI use.
It improves it.
A good prompt is still useful.
But a good prompt becomes much stronger when it sits inside a clear workflow.

title: AI Systems Thinking
text:
The Human-AI-Human Workflow
The central pattern in this guide is the Human-AI-Human workflow.
Human frames the problem
        ↓
AI assists with generation, reasoning, analysis, or automation
        ↓
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts
This pattern keeps responsibility in the right place.
The human is not removed from the work.
The human leads the work.
AI contributes support inside the workflow.
The final output is reviewed, interpreted, and owned by the human or team using it.
This is especially important in education, research, data analysis, health, business, leadership, and public communication, where fluent output can look convincing even when it is incomplete or wrong.

title: AI Systems Thinking
section: What Counts as an AI System?
text:
What Counts as an AI System?
An AI system does not have to be complex.
It can be small and practical.
For example:
A learner uses AI to explain a concept,
checks the explanation against course notes,
writes their own summary,
and records what they learned.
That is a simple AI learning system.
Another example:
An analyst gives AI a cleaned results table,
asks for plain-language interpretation,
checks the interpretation against the data,
creates a figure,
and writes a transparent report.
That is an AI-assisted analysis system.
Another example:
A team creates a prompt template,
uses it to classify support requests,
reviews uncertain cases,
tracks errors,
and updates the template over time.
That is an operational AI workflow.
The common feature is not complexity.
The common feature is structure.

title: AI Systems Thinking
section: Core Components of AI Systems Thinking
text:
Core Components of AI Systems Thinking
AI systems thinking includes five core components.
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Framing] --> B[Context and Inputs]
    B --> C[AI Assistance]
    C --> D[Human Review]
    D --> E[Decision or Output]
    E --> F[Documentation and Improvement]
    F --> A

Problem Framing
The human defines what needs to be done and why it matters.
A weakly framed problem produces weak AI support.
A clearly framed problem gives the AI a useful role.


Context and Inputs
AI needs context.
This may include goals, audience, data, examples, constraints, definitions, previous decisions, or quality criteria.
Better context usually leads to better output.


AI Assistance
AI can assist with generation, summarization, comparison, coding, analysis planning, translation, explanation, documentation, and workflow design.
But assistance is not the same as authority.


Human Review
The human checks whether the output is accurate, appropriate, complete, and useful.
Review may include fact-checking, data validation, expert judgment, ethical reflection, or stakeholder review.


Documentation and Improvement
A system improves when people document what worked, what failed, and what should change next time.
This can be as simple as saving a prompt template or as formal as maintaining an evaluation log.

title: AI Systems Thinking
text:
AI Systems Are Socio-Technical Systems
AI systems are not only technical.
They are socio-technical.
This means they include both technology and people.
They include:

users,
learners,
teams,
organizations,
data,
tools,
policies,
incentives,
risks,
decisions,
and consequences.

A model may generate text, code, or analysis.
But people decide where the model is used, what data it sees, what outputs are accepted, and what actions follow.
That is why AI systems thinking must include human responsibility, not only technical capability.

title: AI Systems Thinking
text:
Where Thinking with AI Fits
Thinking with AI and AI Systems are closely related, but they are not identical.
Thinking with AI focuses on how a person uses AI to think better.
It supports questioning, reasoning, reflection, learning, creativity, critique, and decision-making.
AI Systems expands that mindset into workflows that can be repeated, evaluated, documented, and shared.
Thinking with AI
        ↓
Human-AI-Human reasoning loop
        ↓
AI Systems
        ↓
Reusable workflows, evaluation, governance, and impact
In other words, Thinking with AI is the reasoning layer.
AI Systems is the workflow layer.
Both belong together.

title: AI Systems Thinking
text:
A Practical Example
Suppose a learner wants to understand a difficult topic.
A simple AI interaction may look like this:
Explain machine learning in simple terms.
That may produce a useful answer.
But a systems-thinking version is stronger:
Goal: Understand machine learning well enough to explain it to beginners.
Audience: Secondary school or early university learners.
Task: Explain the concept using simple language, one analogy, and one practical example.
Quality check: Avoid exaggeration. Clearly distinguish data, model, training, prediction, and evaluation.
Output: A short teaching note followed by three review questions.
The second version gives the AI a role inside a learning workflow.
It defines the goal.
It defines the audience.
It defines quality expectations.
It defines the output.
The learner can then review, revise, and save the final explanation.
That is AI systems thinking in practice.

title: AI Systems Thinking
text:
Common Failure Modes
AI systems thinking helps reduce common failures.








Failure mode
What happens
Systems response




Vague prompting
Output is generic
Clarify goal, audience, and constraints


Over-trust
Fluent output is accepted too quickly
Add review and evidence checks


No documentation
Good workflows are lost
Save prompts, decisions, and outputs


No evaluation
Errors repeat
Track quality and failure patterns


Human removed too early
AI output becomes unowned
Keep human review and responsibility


Tool chasing
Focus shifts to novelty
Return to the problem and workflow



The goal is not to make AI perfect.
The goal is to design workflows where AI can be useful without removing judgment.

title: AI Systems Thinking
text:
CDI Working Principle
In CDI guides, AI is treated as a capability inside a broader data, learning, and decision system.
The working principle is:
AI should make human work clearer, faster, more reflective, and more reproducible.
It should not replace thinking, evidence, accountability, or communication.
This principle will guide the rest of the book.

title: AI Systems Thinking
text:
Chapter Summary
AI systems thinking moves us beyond isolated AI prompts.
It asks us to design workflows where AI has a clear role, humans remain responsible, and outputs can be reviewed and improved.
The Human-AI-Human workflow is the central pattern:
Human frames the problem.
AI assists.
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts.
This chapter also positioned Thinking with AI as the reasoning layer and AI Systems as the workflow layer.
Together, they support responsible, reproducible, human-led AI use.

title: AI Systems Thinking
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter introduces AI literacy and responsible use.
We will move from systems thinking into the practical knowledge learners need to understand what AI can do, where it can fail, and how to use it safely and productively.

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
text:
Why AI Literacy Matters
AI literacy is not the same as knowing how to use a chatbot.
A person can write prompts every day and still misunderstand what AI is doing.
A person can receive useful answers from AI and still fail to evaluate whether those answers are accurate, safe, complete, or appropriate for the situation.
AI literacy means understanding AI well enough to use it responsibly.
It means knowing when AI can help.
It means knowing when human judgment matters more.
It means knowing how to ask better questions, evaluate outputs, protect sensitive information, and avoid turning AI assistance into unsupported claims.
In this guide, AI literacy is not treated as a small beginner topic.
It is the foundation for every responsible AI system.
AI systems can generate text, summarize documents, draft code, organize ideas, assist with analysis, and support decision-making workflows.
But AI systems can also produce confident errors.
They can omit important context.
They can reflect bias from training data or user framing.
They can overgeneralize.
They can create outputs that sound polished but are not ready to use.
This is why AI literacy must come before automation.
A weak user with a powerful AI tool can produce weak work faster.
A skilled user with a well-designed workflow can use AI to improve clarity, productivity, reasoning, documentation, and system design.
The difference is not only the tool.
The difference is the human workflow around the tool.
crumbs: 4  AI Literacy and Responsible Use

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
section: AI Literacy Is a Human Skill
text:
AI Literacy Is a Human Skill
AI literacy includes several practical abilities.
These abilities help a person use AI as an assistant rather than as an unchecked authority.
AI literacy includes the ability to:

1. Understand what AI tools are good at.
2. Understand where AI tools can fail.
3. Frame tasks clearly.
4. Provide useful context.
5. Evaluate outputs critically.
6. Protect private and sensitive information.
7. Document how AI was used.
8. Decide when expert review is needed.
This is why AI literacy belongs inside the Human–AI–Human model.
The human begins the process by defining the task.
The AI assists.
The human returns to evaluate, revise, decide, and act.
flowchart LR
    A[Human defines task and context] --> B[AI assists]
    B --> C[Human evaluates output]
    C --> D[Human decides next action]
    D --> E[Documentation and accountability]
AI literacy is the skill that keeps this loop responsible.
crumbs: 4  AI Literacy and Responsible Use

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
text:
What AI Can Do Well
AI tools are useful when the task benefits from language, pattern recognition, structured thinking, summarization, synthesis, or repetitive transformation.
Examples include:
Useful AI-supported tasks:

- Drafting an outline
- Summarizing a document
- Rewriting text for clarity
- Creating checklists
- Explaining unfamiliar concepts
- Brainstorming alternatives
- Generating first-pass code
- Reviewing code structure
- Turning notes into documentation
- Comparing options
- Designing workflows
- Creating templates
These are powerful uses.
But most of them are not final-answer tasks.
They are support tasks.
AI can accelerate the work, but the human remains responsible for whether the output is correct, complete, ethical, and appropriate.
crumbs: 4  AI Literacy and Responsible Use

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
text:
What AI Does Not Guarantee
AI output should not be treated as guaranteed truth.
AI does not automatically guarantee:
AI does not guarantee:

- factual accuracy
- completeness
- fairness
- privacy protection
- legal correctness
- medical correctness
- financial correctness
- statistical validity
- source reliability
- reproducibility
- suitability for a specific audience
This does not make AI useless.
It means AI must be used inside a system that includes evaluation.
In CDI guides, we treat AI output like any other analytical output.
It must be inspected.
It must be tested.
It must be interpreted in context.
It must be documented before it becomes part of a decision.
crumbs: 4  AI Literacy and Responsible Use

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
section: Responsible Use Starts With Task Classification
text:
Responsible Use Starts With Task Classification
Not every AI task carries the same level of risk.
A low-risk task may only need a quick review.
A high-risk task may require source verification, expert review, privacy checks, and formal documentation.
One practical habit is to classify the task before using AI.
Low-risk tasks:
- brainstorming titles
- improving grammar
- summarizing personal notes
- drafting non-sensitive outlines

Moderate-risk tasks:
- drafting public educational content
- creating code for analysis
- interpreting a dataset
- preparing internal documentation

High-risk tasks:
- medical, legal, or financial advice
- decisions affecting people
- clinical interpretation
- hiring or evaluation decisions
- use of private or sensitive data
- claims that require strong evidence
The higher the risk, the stronger the review process must be.
crumbs: 4  AI Literacy and Responsible Use

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
text:
The Responsible AI Use Checklist
Before using AI output, ask the following questions.
Responsible AI use checklist:

[ ] Did I define the task clearly?
[ ] Did I provide enough context?
[ ] Did I avoid sharing sensitive information unnecessarily?
[ ] Did I check the output for factual errors?
[ ] Did I check whether sources are needed?
[ ] Did I check whether expert review is required?
[ ] Did I revise the output for my actual audience?
[ ] Did I document how AI was used when appropriate?
[ ] Did I remain responsible for the final decision?
This checklist is simple, but it changes how AI is used.
It moves the user from passive acceptance to active evaluation.
crumbs: 4  AI Literacy and Responsible Use

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
text:
Privacy and Sensitive Information
Responsible AI use requires careful handling of private and sensitive information.
A practical rule is to avoid sharing unnecessary details.
Instead of pasting sensitive raw information into an AI tool, consider whether the task can be reframed using synthetic, anonymized, or summarized data.
Unsafe pattern:
Paste private records directly into an AI tool without considering privacy.

Safer pattern:
Remove identifiers, summarize the structure, or use synthetic examples when the real details are not needed.
This is especially important for clinical, educational, financial, employment, and personal data.
In AI Systems, privacy is not an afterthought.
It is part of workflow design.
crumbs: 4  AI Literacy and Responsible Use

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
section: From AI Literacy to AI Systems
text:
From AI Literacy to AI Systems
AI literacy begins with individual responsibility.
AI systems extend that responsibility into repeatable workflows.
The difference looks like this:
AI literacy question:
Can I use this AI tool responsibly for this task?

AI systems question:
Can this AI-supported workflow be repeated, evaluated, documented, and improved?
This distinction matters.
A literate AI user can produce better individual outputs.
A well-designed AI system can produce better organizational workflows.
The goal of this guide is to move from the first to the second.
crumbs: 4  AI Literacy and Responsible Use

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
text:
Common Failure Modes
Many AI problems are not caused by the model alone.
They are caused by poor workflow design.
Common failure modes include:
Common failure modes:

1. Unclear task framing
2. Missing context
3. Overtrusting fluent output
4. No evaluation step
5. No documentation
6. No privacy review
7. No human ownership
8. Using AI for a task that requires expert judgment
9. Automating before understanding the workflow
10. Treating AI output as a finished product
These failure modes are preventable.
The solution is not to avoid AI.
The solution is to design better human-led systems around AI.
crumbs: 4  AI Literacy and Responsible Use

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
section: Practical Example: Rewriting an Output Responsibly
text:
Practical Example: Rewriting an Output Responsibly
Imagine a learner asks AI to summarize a technical article.
A weak workflow might look like this:
Prompt:
Summarize this article.

Action:
Copy the AI summary into a report.
A stronger workflow looks like this:
Prompt:
Summarize this article for a beginner audience.
Highlight the main claim, key evidence, limitations, and any terms that need explanation.

Review:
Compare the summary with the original article.
Check whether limitations were included.
Revise unclear language.
Add citation or source information.
Document that AI was used for first-pass summarization.
The tool may be the same.
The system is different.
The second workflow is more responsible because it includes context, evaluation, revision, and documentation.
crumbs: 4  AI Literacy and Responsible Use

title: AI Literacy and Responsible Use
text:
Key Takeaways
AI literacy is the foundation of responsible AI systems.
AI can assist with many tasks, but it does not remove the need for human judgment.
Responsible use requires task framing, privacy awareness, output evaluation, and documentation.
The Human–AI–Human workflow helps keep AI use human-led.
The next step is to move from responsible use into problem framing, where we define what should and should not become an AI-supported task.

We will move from responsible use into the practical skill of deciding what problem is being solved, who the workflow serves, what success looks like, and whether AI is appropriate for the task.
This is where AI work begins to shift from tool use into system design.

title: Problem Framing for AI
text:
Learning Outcomes
AI systems do not begin with prompts.
They begin with problems.
Before a model is asked to summarize, generate, classify, analyze, automate, or recommend, a human must decide what problem is being addressed, why it matters, who will use the result, and how success will be judged.
This chapter focuses on problem framing for AI.
Problem framing is the step where a broad need is converted into a clear, bounded, responsible, and evaluable AI task.
It is where the Human–AI–Human workflow begins.
If the first human step is weak, the rest of the workflow becomes unstable.
A vague problem produces vague prompts.
A vague prompt produces unreliable outputs.
Unreliable outputs create confusion, rework, or risk.
The goal of this chapter is to help you slow down before using AI and define the work clearly enough that AI assistance becomes useful, reviewable, and responsible.
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

title: Problem Framing for AI
Problem Framing for AI
text:
Why Problem Framing Matters
Many AI failures are not model failures.
They are framing failures.
The user asks the wrong question.
The context is incomplete.
The expected output is unclear.
The audience is not defined.
The risk level is underestimated.
The result is used for a decision it was not designed to support.
AI tools can produce fluent answers even when the task is poorly framed. That fluency can hide uncertainty, missing context, or weak assumptions.
Problem framing protects the workflow by making the human intention explicit before AI enters the process.
In CDI terms, problem framing turns AI use from an isolated interaction into part of a system.

title: Problem Framing for AI
section: From Broad Goal to AI-Ready Task
text:
From Broad Goal to AI-Ready Task
A broad goal is usually too large for direct AI use.
For example:
Help me improve this report.
This may be useful as a conversation starter, but it is not yet an AI-ready task.
A better AI-ready task is more specific:
Review this report introduction for clarity, audience fit, and logical flow.
Do not rewrite the technical findings.
Return three sections:
1. strengths,
2. unclear areas,
3. suggested revision priorities.
The second version gives the AI a defined role, a scope, constraints, and an expected output format.
The human still decides what to accept.

title: Problem Framing for AI
text:
The CDI Problem-Framing Pattern
A practical AI problem statement should answer seven questions.
1. What is the real-world problem?
2. Who is the intended user or audience?
3. What input will the AI receive?
4. What should the AI produce?
5. What should the AI not do?
6. How will the output be evaluated?
7. What human decision or action follows?
These questions keep AI work connected to purpose, context, and review.
They also make the task easier to reproduce later.

title: Problem Framing for AI
text:
Example: Weak vs Strong Framing
Weak framing:
Use AI to analyze survey responses.
This is too broad.
It does not say what kind of analysis is needed, what the survey is about, what outputs are expected, or how the results will be used.
Stronger framing:
We have short-text survey responses from training participants.
The goal is to identify common themes about what learners found useful and what they found confusing.
The AI should group responses into themes, provide short labels, and include representative examples.
The AI should not infer demographic information or make claims beyond the provided responses.
A human reviewer will check the themes before they are included in the final report.
This version is clearer because it defines the purpose, input, output, limits, and review step.

title: Problem Framing for AI
Problem Framing for AI
text:
AI Task Risk Levels
A useful CDI AI workflow separates low-risk tasks from higher-risk tasks.
Low risk
- brainstorming titles
- summarizing non-sensitive public text
- improving grammar
- generating study questions

Moderate risk
- summarizing internal notes
- drafting professional communication
- extracting information from documents
- preparing analytical interpretations

High risk
- medical, legal, financial, or safety-related advice
- decisions affecting people, eligibility, employment, or access
- automated judgments without human review
- use of sensitive or private data
Higher-risk tasks require stronger constraints, better documentation, and more human review.
In some cases, AI should support the workflow but not produce the final answer alone.

title: Problem Framing for AI
text:
The Human Decision Context
Every AI task should be connected to a human decision context.
Ask:
What will someone do with the AI output?
Will they publish it?
Will they send it to someone?
Will they use it to make a decision?
Will they use it to prioritize work?
Will they use it to interpret data?
The more consequential the downstream action, the more careful the problem framing must be.
The AI output is not the endpoint.
The human action after the AI output is the endpoint.

title: Problem Framing for AI
text:
A Reusable Problem-Framing Template
Use this template before building a prompt or workflow.
Project or workflow name:

Problem:
What real-world problem are we trying to address?

Audience or user:
Who will use the output?

AI task type:
Is this summarization, drafting, extraction, classification, analysis, automation, or decision support?

Input:
What information will be provided to the AI?

Expected output:
What should the AI return?

Boundaries:
What should the AI avoid doing?

Risk level:
Is this low, moderate, or high risk?

Evaluation:
How will we check whether the output is good enough?

Human review:
Who reviews the output before it is used?

Final human action:
What decision, communication, report, or workflow step follows?
This template can be saved as part of project documentation.
It can also be used before creating prompt libraries, automation scripts, or AI-assisted reports.

title: Problem Framing for AI
text:
Problem Framing Before Prompting
Prompt design is easier after problem framing.
Without framing, prompt writing becomes guesswork.
With framing, the prompt can inherit the structure of the task.
For example, a framed task can be converted into a prompt like this:
Role:
You are helping review short-text survey responses from a training workshop.

Task:
Identify common themes about what learners found useful and what they found confusing.

Input:
The responses are provided below.

Output format:
Return a table with theme, description, example response, and reviewer note.

Boundaries:
Do not infer demographic information.
Do not make claims beyond the provided text.

Review expectation:
The output will be checked by a human before reporting.
The prompt becomes stronger because the problem was framed first.

title: Problem Framing for AI
text:
Common Framing Mistakes
Common mistakes include:
Mistake 1: Asking AI to solve a problem before defining the problem.
Mistake 2: Giving AI a task without identifying the audience.
Mistake 3: Asking for analysis without explaining how the result will be used.
Mistake 4: Treating a high-risk decision as a simple writing task.
Mistake 5: Forgetting to define what the AI should not do.
Mistake 6: Skipping human review because the output sounds confident.
These mistakes are preventable.
The solution is not necessarily a more complex prompt.
The solution is a clearer problem frame.

title: Problem Framing for AI
section: Mini Case Study: Framing an AI Reporting Task
text:
Mini Case Study: Framing an AI Reporting Task
Suppose a team wants to use AI to help prepare a monthly project update.
A weak request might be:
Write a project update using these notes.
A stronger problem frame might be:
Problem:
The team needs a concise monthly project update for internal stakeholders.

Audience:
Project leads and collaborators who need progress, blockers, and next steps.

Input:
Meeting notes, task list, and recent completed activities.

Expected output:
A structured update with three sections: progress, blockers, and next steps.

Boundaries:
Do not invent progress that is not in the notes.
Do not assign blame.
Do not include private comments.

Risk level:
Moderate, because the update may influence stakeholder expectations.

Evaluation:
A human reviewer checks accuracy against the notes before sending.

Final action:
The reviewed update is sent to stakeholders.
This framing makes the AI task clearer, safer, and easier to evaluate.

title: Problem Framing for AI
text:
Practical Exercise
Choose one AI task you currently do or want to do.
Before writing a prompt, complete the problem-framing template.
Then rewrite your original request as an AI-ready task.
Use this structure:
Original request:

Framed problem:

AI-ready task:

Risk level:

Required human review:
The goal is not to make the prompt longer.
The goal is to make the work clearer.

title: Problem Framing for AI
text:
Chapter Summary
Problem framing is the first step in responsible AI systems work.
It defines the purpose, audience, input, output, boundaries, evaluation method, and human decision context.
A well-framed AI task is easier to prompt, easier to evaluate, and safer to use.
The Human–AI–Human workflow begins with the human responsibility to define the problem clearly.

title: Problem Framing for AI
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter moves from problem framing to prompt and context design.
We will use the structure developed here to build clearer prompts, provide better context, define useful constraints, and prepare AI interactions that can be reviewed and reused.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Chapter Purpose
AI systems do not begin with prompts.
They begin with intent.
A prompt is only the visible instruction given to an AI tool. Behind every useful prompt is a human decision about the task, the audience, the context, the constraints, the expected output, and the level of review required.
This chapter introduces prompt and context design as a practical system skill.
The goal is not to memorize prompt formulas.
The goal is to design instructions that help AI tools produce outputs that are useful, reviewable, and aligned with the human purpose of the work.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

Explain why prompts are only one part of an AI workflow.
Distinguish between a weak prompt and a well-framed task instruction.
Identify the types of context an AI system needs to produce useful outputs.
Use a reusable prompt structure for analysis, writing, summarization, planning, and decision support.
Design prompts that make outputs easier to evaluate.
Apply the Human–AI–Human workflow to prompt design.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Prompting Is Not Magic
A weak AI workflow treats prompting as trial and error.
The user asks something vague.
The AI gives a broad answer.
The user asks again.
The AI guesses again.
This may work for casual exploration, but it is not enough for reliable work.
In a CDI AI Systems workflow, prompting is not magic. It is structured communication.
A good prompt tells the AI system:

what task is being performed,
why the task matters,
what context should be used,
what constraints must be respected,
what form the output should take,
and how the output will be reviewed.

The better the task design, the easier the output is to evaluate.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
The Human–AI–Human Prompting Loop
Prompt and context design follows the Human–AI–Human pattern.
Human frames the task and supplies context
        ↓
AI generates, analyzes, reorganizes, or drafts
        ↓
Human evaluates, edits, verifies, and decides
This loop matters because the AI output is not the final authority.
The output is a working artifact.
The human remains responsible for deciding whether the output is correct, useful, ethical, and appropriate for the situation.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
From Question to Task
Many weak prompts are weak because they are written as questions instead of tasks.
A question asks for an answer.
A task defines work to be done.
For example:
Weak question:
Explain AI governance.

Better task:
Explain AI governance for a beginner audience in 5 short sections. Focus on why governance matters in organizations that use AI for analysis, writing, decision support, and automation. Avoid legal jargon. End with a practical checklist.
The second version gives the AI system a clearer job.
It defines the audience, scope, structure, tone, and expected output.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
The CDI Prompt Structure
A reusable prompt structure can help make AI work more consistent.
The following structure can be adapted across many tasks.
Task:
What should the AI do?

Context:
What background information should the AI use?

Audience:
Who is the output for?

Constraints:
What should the AI avoid, include, or respect?

Output format:
What should the result look like?

Review criteria:
How will the human judge whether the output is good enough?
This structure is not a rigid formula.
It is a thinking aid.
It helps the human decide what information is needed before asking the AI to act.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Example: Rewriting a Technical Explanation
A vague prompt may produce a generic result.
Rewrite this more clearly.
A stronger prompt gives context and review criteria.
Task:
Rewrite the following technical explanation so that it is clear to a non-technical project manager.

Context:
The explanation describes why AI-generated summaries must be reviewed before being used in a report.

Audience:
A project manager who understands business workflows but does not have a machine learning background.

Constraints:
Use plain language. Do not overpromise AI accuracy. Keep the explanation under 200 words.

Output format:
Return one revised paragraph followed by three bullet points.

Review criteria:
The output should be accurate, easy to understand, and clear about the need for human review.
This prompt is easier to evaluate because the expectations are explicit.

title: Prompt and Context Design
section: Context Is Part of the System
text:
Context Is Part of the System
AI tools can only respond based on the information available to them.
When context is missing, the AI may fill gaps with assumptions.
Sometimes those assumptions are useful.
Sometimes they are wrong.
Context design asks: what does the AI need to know to complete this task well?
Useful context may include:

project purpose,
target audience,
definitions,
examples,
source material,
data tables,
constraints,
prior decisions,
formatting expectations,
and quality standards.

The goal is not to overload the AI with everything.
The goal is to provide the context that changes the quality of the answer.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Types of Context
Different AI tasks require different types of context.








Context type
Purpose
Example




Task context
Defines the work
“Summarize this meeting transcript into decisions and action items.”


Domain context
Defines the subject area
“This is for a clinical data management workflow.”


Audience context
Defines the reader
“Write for public health analysts, not software engineers.”


Source context
Grounds the answer
“Use only the text provided below.”


Style context
Shapes delivery
“Use a CDI guide tone: clear, structured, practical.”


Constraint context
Sets boundaries
“Do not include claims not supported by the source text.”


Evaluation context
Makes review possible
“Flag uncertain points separately.”



When AI output quality is poor, missing context is often the reason.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Prompt Design for Reviewable Outputs
A good AI output is not only useful.
It is reviewable.
Reviewable outputs make it easy for the human to inspect, correct, and decide.
A prompt can improve reviewability by asking the AI to separate different types of content.
For example:
Return the result in four sections:

1. Summary
2. Key assumptions
3. Uncertain points
4. Recommended next steps
This structure makes it easier to see what the AI is claiming, what it is assuming, and what still needs human judgment.

title: Prompt and Context Design
section: Prompt Design for Data and Analysis Workflows
text:
Prompt Design for Data and Analysis Workflows
In analytical workflows, prompts should be especially careful.
AI can help describe datasets, plan analysis steps, interpret outputs, generate code drafts, and explain results.
But it should not replace verification.
A stronger analytical prompt may look like this:
Task:
Review this results table and suggest possible interpretation themes.

Context:
The table contains differential results from a biological analysis. Columns include feature_id, log2fc, p_value, adjusted_p_value, and annotation.

Constraints:
Do not claim biological significance based only on p-values. Separate observations from interpretations. Flag any missing information needed for stronger conclusions.

Output format:
Return a table with columns: theme, supporting rows, interpretation caution, and next validation step.

Review criteria:
The output should help a human analyst decide what to inspect next, not make final biological claims.
This reflects the CDI pattern: AI assists analysis, but the human analyst remains responsible for interpretation.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Prompt Design for Automation
Automation increases the need for careful prompt design.
A prompt used once can be flexible.
A prompt used repeatedly must be more stable.
For repeated workflows, document:

the task name,
the prompt version,
the input requirements,
the expected output format,
the review process,
and known limitations.

This is why prompt registries are useful in AI systems.
They turn informal prompting into a documented workflow component.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Example Prompt Registry Entry
A prompt registry does not need to be complex at the beginning.
It can start as a simple table.
prompt_id: AI-PROMPT-001
name: Summarize source document
version: 1.0
purpose: Convert a source document into a structured summary for human review
required_input: Source text or document excerpt
output_format: Summary, key points, uncertainties, suggested next steps
review_required: Yes
risk_level: Medium
notes: Do not use for final publication without human verification
This makes the prompt easier to reuse, improve, and audit.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Common Prompt Design Mistakes
Several mistakes appear repeatedly in AI workflows.








Mistake
Why it causes problems
Better practice




Asking vague questions
Produces broad or generic answers
Define a task and output format


Missing audience
Tone and depth may be wrong
State who the output is for


Missing source limits
AI may add unsupported claims
Specify what sources may be used


No constraints
Output may be too long, risky, or unfocused
State boundaries clearly


No review criteria
Hard to judge quality
Define what a good output looks like


Treating output as final
Increases risk of errors
Use human review before action



Prompt design improves when the human becomes clear about the work.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Practical Checklist
Before using AI for a meaningful task, ask:

What exactly do I want the AI to do?
What context does the AI need?
What should the AI avoid?
Who is the output for?
What format will make the output easiest to review?
What would make this output unacceptable?
What must a human verify before using it?

These questions turn prompting into a system habit.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Supporting Script
This guide includes a simple prompt registry script that can summarize prompts from an example AI task log.
Run it from the project root after creating the example data.
bash scripts/bash/01-create-example-ai-task-log.sh

python scripts/python/03-build-prompt-registry.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results
Expected output:
data/results/prompt-registry.csv
The script is intentionally simple. Its purpose is to demonstrate the system idea: prompts can be documented, summarized, reviewed, and improved over time.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Chapter Summary
Prompt and context design is not about finding magic words.
It is about designing clear human instructions for AI-assisted work.
A good prompt defines the task, supplies relevant context, sets constraints, requests a reviewable output, and supports human judgment.
In CDI AI Systems, prompts are not isolated tricks.
They are reusable workflow components inside a Human–AI–Human system.

title: Prompt and Context Design
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter focuses on data and knowledge grounding.
We will move from designing better prompts to ensuring that AI outputs are connected to appropriate source material, project context, evidence, and documented knowledge.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Chapter Purpose
AI systems are most useful when they are connected to the right context.
A prompt can ask a clear question.
But a clear question is not enough if the AI system does not have the information needed to answer well.
When source material, data, definitions, prior decisions, and constraints are missing, the AI may rely on general patterns instead of project-specific evidence.
That can produce fluent but unsupported output.
This chapter introduces data and knowledge grounding as a core AI systems skill.
Grounding means connecting AI work to the information that should shape the answer.
In a CDI workflow, grounding helps move AI use from casual assistance toward reproducible, reviewable, and defensible work.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

Explain what grounding means in an AI workflow.
Distinguish between general AI knowledge, user-provided context, project data, and authoritative sources.
Identify when an AI output is insufficiently grounded.
Design grounded prompts that separate source-based claims from interpretation.
Use simple project files to organize AI-ready knowledge.
Apply the Human–AI–Human workflow to source review, evidence use, and documentation.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
What Grounding Means
Grounding means linking an AI task to relevant evidence or context.
In practical work, grounding may include:

a dataset,
a table of results,
a report,
a protocol,
meeting notes,
a style guide,
a project decision log,
a reference document,
a glossary,
or a trusted external source.

The purpose is simple.
The AI should not have to guess the project context.
The human supplies or identifies the information that matters.
Then the AI assists within that context.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
The Human–AI–Human Grounding Loop
Grounding follows the Human–AI–Human workflow.
Human selects the relevant sources and context
        ↓
AI summarizes, extracts, compares, reorganizes, or drafts
        ↓
Human verifies claims against the sources and decides what to use
The human role is especially important at the beginning and the end.
At the beginning, the human decides what counts as relevant source material.
At the end, the human checks whether the AI output is faithful to that material.
AI can assist with evidence organization.
It should not silently replace evidence judgment.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
section: Why Ungrounded AI Outputs Are Risky
text:
Why Ungrounded AI Outputs Are Risky
AI-generated text can sound confident even when it is weakly supported.
This is one of the main reasons grounding matters.
An ungrounded AI output may:

invent details,
overgeneralize,
miss project-specific constraints,
mix facts with assumptions,
cite unclear evidence,
or produce recommendations that do not fit the actual situation.

In low-risk brainstorming, this may be acceptable.
In analysis, reporting, education, governance, health, finance, or organizational decision-making, it is not enough.
The stronger the consequence of the output, the stronger the grounding and review process should be.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Types of Grounding
Not all grounding is the same.
Different tasks require different forms of context.








Grounding type
What it provides
Example




Task grounding
Defines the work
“Extract action items from these meeting notes.”


Source grounding
Limits claims to provided material
“Use only the attached report.”


Data grounding
Connects output to structured data
“Interpret this results table.”


Domain grounding
Adds subject-matter context
“This is for a clinical data workflow.”


Decision grounding
Connects work to prior choices
“Use the approved inclusion criteria.”


Style grounding
Aligns presentation
“Write in CDI guide style.”


Governance grounding
Adds rules and constraints
“Do not include confidential information.”



A strong AI workflow makes the grounding type explicit.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
General Knowledge Is Not Enough
Large AI models can provide useful general explanations.
But general knowledge is not the same as project knowledge.
For example, an AI tool may explain what evaluation means in machine learning.
That does not mean it knows:

which dataset your project uses,
which metric your team selected,
which tradeoffs were accepted,
which assumptions were documented,
which audience will read the report,
or which decisions depend on the output.

General AI knowledge can support understanding.
Project-specific knowledge must come from the project.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Source-Grounded Prompts
A source-grounded prompt gives the AI a clear boundary.
For example:
Task:
Summarize the source text below into key decisions, open questions, and next steps.

Source boundary:
Use only the source text provided. Do not add external facts.

Output format:
Return three sections: Decisions, Open Questions, Next Steps.

Review rule:
If a point is not clearly supported by the source text, place it under Open Questions instead of presenting it as a decision.
This prompt does not only ask for a summary.
It defines what evidence is allowed.
It also gives the AI a way to handle uncertainty.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Data-Grounded Prompts
Data-grounded prompts are especially important in analytical workflows.
The AI should know what the table represents, what columns mean, and what it should avoid claiming.
For example:
Task:
Review the following AI evaluation summary table and identify quality patterns.

Data context:
Each row represents an AI-assisted task. Columns include task_id, task_type, risk_level, output_quality_score, review_status, and reviewer_notes.

Constraints:
Do not make claims beyond the table. Do not treat a quality score as proof of correctness. Separate observations from recommendations.

Output format:
Return a table with columns: observation, supporting_field, possible_interpretation, recommended_human_review.
This helps keep the AI output connected to the actual data.
It also prevents the AI from presenting unsupported conclusions as facts.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Knowledge Grounding with Project Files
A practical AI system often needs a small knowledge structure.
This does not need to be complex at the beginning.
A simple project can include:
data/
├── example/
│   └── ai-task-log.csv
├── input/
│   └── project-context.md
├── results/
│   ├── ai-evaluation-summary.csv
│   └── prompt-registry.csv
└── reports/
    └── ai-evaluation-summary.png
The goal is to make context reusable.
Instead of rewriting the same background every time, the project can maintain stable files that describe the work.
Examples include:

a project overview,
a glossary,
a task registry,
a prompt registry,
an evaluation rubric,
and a decision log.

These files become the memory of the workflow.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Example Project Context File
A simple project context file can help ground AI work.
Project name:
CDI AI Systems Guide

Purpose:
Teach learners how to design responsible, reproducible, human-led AI workflows.

Core workflow:
Human defines the problem.
AI assists with generation, reasoning, analysis, or automation.
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts.

Audience:
Learners, analysts, educators, researchers, and professionals.

Quality expectations:
Outputs should be clear, reviewable, source-aware, and cautious about unsupported claims.

Boundaries:
Do not treat AI output as final evidence. Do not include confidential information in prompts. Document assumptions and review steps.
This type of file can be reused when drafting chapters, reviewing outputs, designing prompts, or building examples.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Grounded Outputs Should Separate Claims
A grounded AI output should make it clear what kind of statement is being made.
Useful categories include:








Statement type
Meaning
Review need




Source-based observation
Directly supported by provided material
Check against the source


Interpretation
Human or AI explanation of what the observation may mean
Review for logic and context


Assumption
Something not directly proven by the source
Confirm or revise


Recommendation
Suggested next action
Review for feasibility and risk


Uncertainty
Something unclear or missing
Investigate further



This structure prevents a common AI failure: blending evidence, inference, and recommendation into one confident paragraph.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Grounding and Citations
Grounding does not always require formal citations.
But it does require traceability.
Traceability means the human can answer questions such as:

What source was used?
What data table supported the claim?
What assumption was made?
What was generated by AI?
What was verified by a human?
What still needs review?

In public reports, academic writing, technical documentation, or high-stakes contexts, citations and source references become more important.
In internal workflows, a clear decision log or source note may be enough.
The principle is the same: important claims should be traceable.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Knowledge Grounding and Retrieval
Some AI systems use retrieval to bring relevant documents into the model context.
This is often called retrieval-augmented generation.
The idea is straightforward:
User asks a question
        ↓
System retrieves relevant source material
        ↓
AI generates an answer using that material
        ↓
Human reviews the answer and checks the sources
Retrieval can improve grounding, but it does not remove the need for review.
The retrieved material may be incomplete, outdated, irrelevant, or misinterpreted.
The system still needs human evaluation.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Common Grounding Mistakes
Several grounding mistakes appear often in AI workflows.








Mistake
Why it matters
Better practice




No source boundary
AI may add unsupported material
State what sources may be used


Too much context
Important details may be buried
Provide the most relevant context first


Missing definitions
AI may interpret terms differently
Include a glossary or definitions


Unclear data columns
AI may misread the table
Explain column meanings


No uncertainty handling
Weak claims may appear final
Ask AI to flag unclear points


No review trail
Hard to audit later
Keep prompts, outputs, and review notes



Grounding is not only about adding more information.
It is about adding the right information in a reviewable way.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Supporting Workflow
This guide includes example scripts that create and summarize an AI task log.
The task log acts as a simple grounding artifact because it records what AI was used for, what risk level was assigned, and whether review occurred.
Run from the project root:
bash scripts/bash/01-create-example-ai-task-log.sh

python scripts/python/02-evaluate-ai-output-quality.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results

python scripts/python/03-build-prompt-registry.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results
Expected outputs:
data/example/ai-task-log.csv
data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv
data/results/prompt-registry.csv
These outputs are not meant to be a complete AI governance system.
They are starter artifacts for documenting AI-assisted work.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Practical Checklist
Before using AI with source material or project data, ask:

What source or data should ground the output?
What information is allowed?
What information is missing?
What claims must be separated from assumptions?
What should the AI avoid adding?
How will the human verify the output?
Where will the prompt, source, output, and review decision be documented?

These questions make grounding part of the workflow rather than an afterthought.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Chapter Summary
Data and knowledge grounding connects AI work to the information that should shape the output.
Grounding helps reduce unsupported claims, improves reviewability, and makes AI-assisted workflows more reproducible.
In CDI AI Systems, grounding is part of responsible Human–AI–Human work.
The human selects the relevant sources.
AI assists with organization, extraction, summarization, drafting, or analysis.
The human verifies, documents, and decides what can be used.

title: Data and Knowledge Grounding
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter focuses on evaluation and quality control.
We will move from grounding AI outputs to judging whether those outputs are accurate, useful, safe, complete, and ready for human-approved use.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Why Evaluation Matters
AI systems are not complete when an answer is generated.
They are complete only when the output has been reviewed, tested, documented, and placed in the correct human decision context.
This is one of the most important differences between casual AI use and AI systems work.
Casual AI use often stops at the first useful-looking answer.
AI systems work asks a different question:
Can this output be trusted for this purpose?
That question requires evaluation.
AI tools can produce fluent answers that sound confident even when they are incomplete, unsupported, outdated, biased, or wrong.
This creates a serious workflow risk.
The problem is not only that AI can make mistakes.
Humans can also make mistakes.
The deeper problem is that AI output can look polished before it has been verified.
In CDI-style workflows, evaluation is not optional. It is a required layer between AI assistance and human action.
The evaluation step protects the quality of the final decision.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Human defines the task
        ↓
AI generates or assists
        ↓
Human evaluates the output
        ↓
Human revises, documents, decides, or acts

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Evaluation Is Context-Specific
There is no single universal score that tells us whether an AI output is good.
A useful output depends on the task.
For example, a good brainstorming answer may be:

broad,
creative,
exploratory,
and open-ended.

A good clinical, legal, financial, or scientific answer must be:

grounded,
cautious,
traceable,
current,
and reviewed by appropriate human expertise.

The same AI model can be useful in both settings, but the evaluation standard must change.
That is why every AI workflow should define quality criteria before relying on output.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Common Evaluation Dimensions
A practical AI evaluation workflow can begin with several dimensions.







Dimension
Evaluation question




Accuracy
Is the output factually correct for the task?


Completeness
Does it answer all important parts of the request?


Relevance
Does it stay focused on the user’s actual problem?


Grounding
Is the output supported by provided data, sources, or context?


Clarity
Is the output understandable to the intended audience?


Safety
Could the output cause harm if used directly?


Bias
Does the output make unfair, unsupported, or stereotyping assumptions?


Reproducibility
Can the workflow be repeated or audited later?


Actionability
Does the output support a clear next step?



These dimensions do not all have equal importance in every workflow.
A writing assistant may emphasize clarity and tone.
A data workflow may emphasize reproducibility, grounding, and traceability.
A high-stakes decision workflow may emphasize safety, expert review, and documented limitations.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
section: A Simple AI Output Review Checklist
text:
A Simple AI Output Review Checklist
Before using an AI-generated output, ask:

What task was the AI asked to perform?
What information did the AI use?
What information was missing?
What claims need verification?
What parts are useful as-is?
What parts require editing or correction?
What risks would appear if this output were used directly?
Who needs to review this before action?
What should be documented?
What should not be trusted without additional evidence?

This checklist turns evaluation into a habit.
It also reinforces the Human–AI–Human workflow.
AI assists, but humans remain responsible for judgment, verification, and action.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Task Risk and Evaluation Depth
Not every AI task requires the same level of review.
Low-risk tasks may need light review.
Medium-risk tasks need structured review.
High-risk tasks need expert review, source verification, documentation, and clear accountability.








Task risk
Example
Evaluation depth




Low
Rewriting a non-sensitive paragraph
Check tone, clarity, and meaning


Medium
Summarizing meeting notes or project plans
Check completeness, omissions, and action items


High
Medical, legal, financial, employment, or public claims
Verify sources, involve expertise, document uncertainty


System-level
Automated workflows or repeated AI outputs
Evaluate outputs over time and monitor failure patterns



The riskier the task, the more important the evaluation layer becomes.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
section: Evaluation as a Workflow, Not a Feeling
text:
Evaluation as a Workflow, Not a Feeling
A common mistake is to judge AI output based on whether it “looks good.”
That is not enough.
A better approach is to make evaluation visible.
For example, an AI task log can record:

the task,
the prompt,
the context used,
the model or tool used,
the output type,
the human reviewer,
quality scores,
revision notes,
and final decision status.

This turns AI use into a documented workflow.
That documentation is especially important when AI becomes part of repeated work.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Example AI Task Log
The scaffolded guide includes a small example dataset for AI task evaluation.
The file is created by:
bash scripts/bash/01-create-example-ai-task-log.sh
The expected output is:
data/example/ai-task-log.csv
A simplified version of the task log may include columns like:
task_id,task_type,risk_level,prompt_version,output_reviewed,accuracy_score,clarity_score,grounding_score,human_decision
This does not evaluate the AI model in a laboratory sense.
Instead, it evaluates the usefulness and reliability of AI outputs inside a human workflow.
That is the level of evaluation most applied AI users need first.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
section: Supporting Script: Evaluate AI Output Quality
text:
Supporting Script: Evaluate AI Output Quality
The scaffold includes a Python script for summarizing evaluation scores.
Run it with:
python scripts/python/02-evaluate-ai-output-quality.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results
Expected output:
data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv
This script is intentionally simple.
It demonstrates how a project can move from informal AI review into a structured quality-control table.
In a larger AI system, this same idea can be extended to include:

reviewer agreement,
task-level pass/fail criteria,
source checks,
hallucination flags,
fairness checks,
and revision history.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Supporting Script: Plot Evaluation Summary
The scaffold also includes an R script for creating a visual summary.
Run it with:
Rscript scripts/R/04-plot-evaluation-summary.R \
  data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv \
  data/reports
Expected output:
data/reports/ai-evaluation-summary.png
The plot is not meant to prove that an AI system is safe or correct.
It is meant to make review patterns visible.
Visibility is the beginning of quality control.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Evaluation Failure Modes
AI evaluation can fail in predictable ways.







Failure mode
What happens




Fluency bias
A polished answer is mistaken for a correct answer


Confirmation bias
The reviewer accepts outputs that match prior beliefs


Missing context
The AI responds without important background information


Weak grounding
The output makes claims that are not supported by sources or data


Unclear task criteria
Nobody defined what a good output should look like


No review record
The final decision cannot be audited later


Automation drift
A workflow works once but fails when repeated in new contexts



The goal of evaluation is not perfection.
The goal is controlled, visible, accountable improvement.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
section: Human Review Is Not a Formality
text:
Human Review Is Not a Formality
Human review is not just a final glance.
It is an active decision step.
A reviewer should be able to say:

what was accepted,
what was changed,
what was rejected,
what remains uncertain,
and why the final output is suitable for its intended use.

This is especially important when AI output is used in reports, public communication, education, research, business decisions, or health-related contexts.
Human review is where responsibility re-enters the workflow.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Practical Evaluation Template
A reusable AI output evaluation note can look like this:
Task:

AI tool or model used:

Input context provided:

Output reviewed by:

Accuracy notes:

Grounding notes:

Clarity notes:

Safety or risk concerns:

Required revisions:

Final decision:

Limitations:
This can be used as a lightweight review record.
For higher-stakes work, the template should be expanded with source links, reviewer names, version numbers, and approval status.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
CDI Principle
A CDI AI workflow should not ask only:
Did the AI produce an answer?
It should ask:
Was the answer evaluated well enough for the decision it supports?
This principle keeps AI work connected to data, insight, and impact.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Chapter Summary
In this chapter, we introduced AI evaluation and quality control as essential parts of AI systems work.
We distinguished casual output acceptance from structured review.
We defined common evaluation dimensions such as accuracy, completeness, grounding, clarity, safety, bias, reproducibility, and actionability.
We also introduced simple supporting scripts for summarizing and visualizing AI task evaluation records.
Most importantly, we reinforced the Human–AI–Human workflow:
Human defines the task.
AI assists.
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts.
Evaluation is the bridge between AI output and responsible use.

title: Evaluation and Quality Control
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter moves from evaluation into human-in-the-loop workflows.
We will examine how to design AI-supported processes where humans remain involved at the right points: before the task, during review, during revision, and before final action.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Learning Outcomes
AI systems should not be designed as pipelines where humans disappear.
They should be designed as workflows where humans remain responsible for framing the problem, checking the output, making decisions, and documenting what was done.
This is especially important when AI is used for analysis, writing, decision support, automation, teaching, research, business operations, or public communication.
AI can assist.
AI can accelerate.
AI can summarize, classify, draft, compare, suggest, organize, and automate.
But the human remains responsible for the meaning, quality, ethics, and consequences of the work.
That is the purpose of human-in-the-loop workflow design.
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Explain why human review is central to responsible AI systems.
Identify where human judgment is needed in an AI workflow.
Distinguish low-risk review from high-risk review.
Design a Human-AI-Human workflow for a real task.
Create checkpoints for quality, safety, accuracy, and accountability.
Document AI-assisted work in a way that is reviewable and reproducible.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
The Human-AI-Human Workflow
The central model of this guide is the Human-AI-Human workflow.
Human defines the problem
        ↓
AI assists with generation, reasoning, analysis, or automation
        ↓
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts
This model prevents AI from becoming an unreviewed black box.
It also prevents the human from using AI as a substitute for thinking.
The value of AI is strongest when human judgment and machine assistance are combined deliberately.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Why Human Review Matters
AI outputs can appear fluent even when they are incomplete, inaccurate, poorly grounded, or inappropriate for the task.
Human review matters because AI systems may:

Omit important context.
Invent unsupported details.
Misinterpret the task.
Overgeneralize from weak evidence.
Produce confident but incorrect answers.
Miss ethical, legal, cultural, or organizational constraints.
Generate outputs that sound polished but are not defensible.

A responsible workflow does not ask, “Did AI produce something?”
It asks, “Has a human reviewed whether this output is useful, accurate, appropriate, and safe for its intended purpose?”

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
section: Where Humans Belong in the Workflow
text:
Where Humans Belong in the Workflow
Human oversight is not only needed at the end.
It belongs throughout the workflow.
Problem framing
  Human decides what the task is and why it matters.

Context preparation
  Human selects the background information, data, constraints, and sources.

AI assistance
  AI generates, summarizes, analyzes, transforms, or proposes outputs.

Review and evaluation
  Human checks accuracy, relevance, grounding, completeness, tone, and risk.

Decision and action
  Human decides what to use, revise, reject, escalate, or document.

Documentation
  Human records the task, prompt, source material, output status, and review outcome.
This structure turns AI use into a workflow rather than a one-off interaction.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Human Judgment Cannot Be Outsourced
AI can help produce options.
It can help compare alternatives.
It can help organize evidence.
It can help draft documentation.
But some responsibilities remain human responsibilities.
These include:

Defining the purpose of the work.
Understanding the audience.
Recognizing sensitive context.
Interpreting consequences.
Knowing when the output is not good enough.
Deciding whether a task should be automated.
Taking responsibility for final action.

A human-in-the-loop system is not weak because it requires review.
It is stronger because it makes responsibility explicit.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Review Depth Depends on Risk
Not every AI output needs the same level of review.
A brainstormed list of possible blog titles does not need the same review as a medical summary, legal memo, financial recommendation, hiring decision, or research interpretation.
A practical AI system should classify tasks by risk.
Low-risk task
  Examples: brainstorming, formatting, rewriting, summarizing non-sensitive notes.
  Review need: quick human review for usefulness and tone.

Moderate-risk task
  Examples: analysis planning, educational materials, public communication, workflow design.
  Review need: careful review for accuracy, completeness, and audience fit.

High-risk task
  Examples: clinical, legal, financial, employment, policy, safety, or sensitive personal decisions.
  Review need: expert review, source verification, documentation, and possible non-AI decision control.
The higher the risk, the more structured the human checkpoint should be.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
A Human Review Checklist
A simple review checklist can prevent many AI workflow failures.
AI Output Review Checklist

Task clarity
[ ] Does the output answer the actual question?
[ ] Does it match the intended audience?
[ ] Does it stay within the requested scope?

Grounding
[ ] Is the output based on the provided data, sources, or context?
[ ] Are unsupported claims removed or flagged?
[ ] Are citations or evidence included where needed?

Accuracy
[ ] Are key facts correct?
[ ] Are numbers, names, dates, and definitions checked?
[ ] Are assumptions clearly stated?

Completeness
[ ] Are important limitations included?
[ ] Are missing data or uncertainty noted?
[ ] Are next steps clear?

Risk and responsibility
[ ] Could this output affect health, money, legal status, safety, employment, or reputation?
[ ] Does it require expert review?
[ ] Is a human clearly responsible for the final decision?
This checklist can be adapted for different CDI systems.
In a data science system, the review may focus on assumptions, metrics, and interpretation.
In a medical data system, the review may focus on privacy, clinical validity, and regulatory risk.
In an AI system, the review focuses on whether AI assistance has been used responsibly inside a human-led workflow.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Designing Human Checkpoints
A checkpoint is a deliberate moment where the workflow pauses for human review.
Good checkpoints are specific.
They do not simply say, “review output.”
They say what to review, who reviews it, what evidence is needed, and what happens next.
Checkpoint name:
  Output quality review

When it occurs:
  After AI produces a draft or analysis summary

Reviewer:
  Human task owner

Review criteria:
  Accuracy, completeness, grounding, tone, and risk level

Possible outcomes:
  Accept, revise, rerun with better context, escalate, or reject

Documentation:
  Record review status, reviewer, date, and notes
This makes the workflow auditable.
It also makes it easier to improve over time.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Human-in-the-Loop Workflow Template
