The following template can be reused when designing AI-assisted workflows.
Workflow name:

Purpose:

Audience or user:

Input materials:

AI role:

Human role before AI use:

Human role after AI use:

Review criteria:

Risk level:

Escalation trigger:

Documentation required:

Final decision owner:
This template is useful before moving into automation.
If a workflow cannot be clearly described with this template, it is probably not ready for automation.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Example: AI-Assisted Report Drafting
Consider a workflow where AI helps draft a short report from a structured results table.
A weak workflow looks like this:
Upload table → ask AI to write report → publish output
This is risky because there is no explicit review step.
A stronger workflow looks like this:
Human defines report purpose
        ↓
Human prepares results table and interpretation notes
        ↓
AI drafts structured report
        ↓
Human checks claims against the table
        ↓
Human revises interpretation and adds limitations
        ↓
Human approves final report
        ↓
Workflow records prompt, source table, output, and review status
The second workflow is slower than the first.
But it is more defensible, reusable, and trustworthy.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Example: AI-Assisted Data Cleaning Suggestions
AI can help suggest data cleaning steps.
But it should not silently alter important data without review.
A responsible workflow may look like this:
Human provides data dictionary and sample records
        ↓
AI identifies possible inconsistencies
        ↓
Human reviews suggested cleaning rules
        ↓
Script applies approved rules
        ↓
Human checks before-and-after summaries
        ↓
Workflow saves cleaning log
Here, AI is useful for pattern recognition and suggestion.
The human remains responsible for approving the rules.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Reviewable Outputs
AI workflows should produce outputs that can be reviewed.
A reviewable output is clear enough for a human to inspect, question, and improve.
Examples include:

A table of extracted claims.
A summary with source references.
A draft with assumptions listed.
A checklist with pass/fail decisions.
A comparison table of options.
A workflow log showing prompt, input, output, and review status.

Avoid outputs that are too vague to inspect.
For example, “looks good” is not a reviewable result.
A better review note is:
Reviewed for factual accuracy, audience fit, and completeness.
Two unsupported claims were removed.
One limitation section was added.
Approved for use in internal training only.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Documentation as Human Oversight
Documentation is part of human oversight.
If an AI-assisted output matters, the workflow should record what happened.
At minimum, useful documentation may include:
Task:
Input material:
Prompt or prompt version:
AI output location:
Reviewer:
Review date:
Review status:
Changes made:
Limitations:
Final decision:
This record does not need to be complicated.
But it should be clear enough that someone else can understand what was done.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Supporting Script Connection
The task log created earlier in this guide can support human-in-the-loop documentation.
For example:
bash scripts/bash/01-create-example-ai-task-log.sh
Then evaluation summaries can be created with:
python scripts/python/02-evaluate-ai-output-quality.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results
These scripts do not replace human judgment.
They help organize review evidence so that human judgment becomes easier to document.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Common Failure Modes
Human-in-the-loop workflows often fail when the review step is vague or performative.
Common failure modes include:
Rubber-stamp review
  Human approves output without checking it carefully.

Unclear ownership
  No one knows who is responsible for the final decision.

No escalation path
  The reviewer finds a problem but does not know what to do next.

No documentation
  The workflow produces output but leaves no record of prompt, input, review, or decision.

Review too late
  Human review happens after the output has already been used.

Automation too early
  A task is automated before quality criteria and review checkpoints are defined.
A good AI system anticipates these failures and designs around them.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Practical Rule
Before using AI output in real work, ask three questions:
Who reviewed this?

What did they check?

Who is responsible for the final decision?
If those questions cannot be answered, the workflow is not yet ready for serious use.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Chapter Summary
Human-in-the-loop design keeps AI systems responsible, reviewable, and aligned with human goals.
The goal is not to slow down AI use unnecessarily.
The goal is to place human judgment where it matters most.
In the CDI AI Systems framework, AI assistance sits inside a human-led workflow.
Humans define the problem.
AI assists.
Humans evaluate, decide, document, and act.
That is the Human-AI-Human workflow in practice.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Workflows
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter introduces automation and agentic workflows.
We will build on the human-in-the-loop foundation by asking when AI-assisted tasks can be safely repeated, scripted, scheduled, or partially automated.
The key message will remain the same: automation should come after clear problem framing, grounding, evaluation, human review, and documented responsibility.

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
text:
Learning Outcomes
Automation is often presented as the goal of AI work.
In practice, automation should not be the starting point.
Before a workflow is automated, the task must be understood. The inputs must be clear. The expected outputs must be defined. The risks must be known. The review points must be visible.
This chapter explains how to move from assisted AI use into structured automation and agentic workflows without losing human judgment, traceability, or accountability.
The CDI approach is simple:
Human-led workflows come first.
Automation comes after clarity.
Agentic behavior comes only after evaluation.
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
Automation and Agentic Workflows
text:
From Assistance to Automation
Most AI use begins as assistance.
A human asks a question, provides context, reviews the result, and decides what to do next.
Automation begins when repeated steps are turned into a reusable workflow.
Agentic workflows go further. They allow AI-enabled tools to plan or execute multiple steps toward a goal, often using tools, memory, files, search, code, or external systems.
These are not the same thing.









Workflow type
Human role
AI role
Risk level




Assisted AI use
Directs each step
Responds to one request at a time
Usually lower


Automated AI workflow
Defines and reviews workflow
Executes repeated steps
Medium


Agentic workflow
Sets goal and constraints
Plans or acts across steps
Higher



The more autonomy an AI workflow has, the more important evaluation, logging, and human oversight become.

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
text:
The Human-AI-Human Model Still Applies
Automation does not remove the human.
It changes where the human appears in the workflow.
Human defines the goal, boundaries, and review criteria
        ↓
AI system performs one or more structured steps
        ↓
Human reviews outputs, decisions, logs, and next actions
This is still the Human-AI-Human workflow.
The human may not manually perform every step, but the human remains responsible for problem framing, quality control, interpretation, and action.

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
Automation and Agentic Workflows
text:
Automation Readiness Checklist
Before automating an AI workflow, ask:







Question
Why it matters




What problem is this workflow solving?
Prevents automating vague tasks


What inputs does it require?
Supports reproducibility


What output should it produce?
Makes evaluation possible


How will quality be checked?
Prevents unreviewed AI output


What should happen when confidence is low?
Supports safe escalation


What must remain human-reviewed?
Preserves accountability


What will be logged?
Supports traceability


What data should not be sent to AI tools?
Protects privacy and confidentiality



This checklist should be completed before moving from manual AI assistance to automation.

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
text:
A Simple AI Automation Pattern
A basic AI automation workflow can be written as a sequence of controlled steps.
1. Load task input
2. Validate required fields
3. Build prompt using approved template
4. Generate AI-assisted output
5. Evaluate output against criteria
6. Save output and evaluation log
7. Flag uncertain or high-risk cases for human review
8. Produce a final reviewable report
This pattern is intentionally conservative.
It does not treat AI output as final by default.
It treats AI output as an intermediate artifact that must be evaluated, documented, and reviewed.

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
text:
Example: Non-Executable Workflow Command
A project might include a workflow script that runs the example AI task log through evaluation and reporting steps.
bash scripts/bash/01-create-example-ai-task-log.sh

python scripts/python/02-evaluate-ai-output-quality.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results

python scripts/python/03-build-prompt-registry.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results

Rscript scripts/R/04-plot-evaluation-summary.R \
  data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv \
  data/reports
In this guide, commands shown inside chapters are examples for the reader. They are not executable Quarto chunks.

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
Automation and Agentic Workflows
text:
Boundaries for Agentic Workflows
Agentic workflows should have clear limits.
These limits include:



Boundary
Example




Task boundary
Only summarize documents in a specified folder


Data boundary
Do not use private or external data unless approved


Tool boundary
Use only approved scripts or read-only tools


Time boundary
Stop after a defined number of steps


Decision boundary
Do not make final decisions without human review


Output boundary
Produce a report, not direct action



The goal is not to prevent useful automation.
The goal is to prevent uncontrolled automation.

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
Automation and Agentic Workflows
section: Failure Modes in Automated AI Workflows
text:
Failure Modes in Automated AI Workflows
Automation can make AI failures harder to see.
Common failure modes include:







Failure mode
What happens




Silent hallucination
False information enters a report without review


Context drift
Later steps no longer match the original task


Over-automation
The system acts where human judgment is needed


Tool misuse
The workflow calls the wrong script, file, or source


Poor logging
Outputs cannot be traced or audited


Privacy leakage
Sensitive data is sent where it should not go


Evaluation gap
Outputs are generated but never checked



These are system design problems, not only model problems.
A better model does not replace a better workflow.

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
Automation and Agentic Workflows
text:
Mini Case Example
Suppose a team uses AI to draft weekly project updates.
A weak workflow would be:
Ask AI to write the update and send it.
A stronger workflow would be:
1. Human provides project notes and audience.
2. AI drafts a structured update.
3. AI checks the draft against a communication checklist.
4. Human reviews accuracy, tone, and missing context.
5. Human edits and sends the final message.
6. The final version and checklist are saved for future improvement.
The second workflow is slower at first.
But it is safer, more reusable, and easier to improve.

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
Automation and Agentic Workflows
text:
Summary
Automation is useful when a workflow is clear, repeated, and reviewable.
Agentic workflows are more powerful, but they require stronger boundaries, logging, and human checkpoints.
The CDI approach does not treat automation as a replacement for human thinking.
It treats automation as a way to make responsible human-led workflows more consistent, scalable, and reproducible.

title: Automation and Agentic Workflows
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter moves from automation into AI-assisted analysis and reporting.
We will focus on how AI can support analytical writing, interpretation, summarization, and report generation while keeping outputs grounded, reviewable, and aligned with human judgment.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
text:
Learning Outcomes
AI can help people move from raw information to clearer analysis, better summaries, cleaner documentation, and more polished reports.
But AI-assisted analysis is not the same as AI-generated truth.
An AI tool can help organize evidence. It can suggest patterns. It can draft explanations. It can create first-pass summaries. It can help translate technical outputs into plain language.
The human remains responsible for checking the evidence, interpreting the results, deciding what claims are justified, and communicating uncertainty honestly.
This chapter explains how to use AI inside analytical and reporting workflows without weakening reproducibility, traceability, or scientific judgment.
The CDI position is simple:
AI can assist analysis.
AI can assist reporting.
But conclusions must remain evidence-led and human-reviewed.
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
text:
Why AI-Assisted Reporting Matters
Many analytical projects fail after the analysis is complete.
The results exist, but they are not communicated clearly.
The tables are correct, but the interpretation is weak.
The plots are useful, but the claims are too broad.
The report is written, but it does not explain what decisions the results support.
AI can help with this translation layer.
It can help turn structured results into readable summaries. It can help create outlines. It can compare draft interpretations against source tables. It can help explain methods for different audiences.
However, this only works well when the AI is grounded in the actual project outputs.
AI should not invent an analysis.
It should help explain an analysis that already exists.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
text:
The Human-AI-Human Reporting Loop
AI-assisted reporting follows the same CDI pattern used throughout this guide.
Human prepares the evidence, question, audience, and reporting goal
        ↓
AI assists with structure, summary, explanation, or draft language
        ↓
Human verifies evidence, revises claims, documents uncertainty, and approves the final report
The human starts with the analytical context.
The AI helps create a draft or supporting explanation.
The human reviews the output against the data and decides what can be said.
This matters because reporting is not only writing.
Reporting is interpretation.
Interpretation requires accountability.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
text:
Analysis, Interpretation, and Communication
AI-assisted reporting becomes safer when these three layers are separated.









Layer
Main question
Human responsibility
AI support




Analysis
What was computed?
Verify data, methods, and outputs
Summarize tables or workflow steps


Interpretation
What does it mean?
Decide what claims are justified
Suggest possible explanations or caveats


Communication
How should it be explained?
Match message to audience and context
Draft summaries, outlines, and plain-language text



Problems happen when AI skips directly from data to conclusion.
A responsible workflow keeps the layers visible.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
section: Appropriate Uses of AI in Analysis Workflows

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
section: Risky Uses of AI in Analysis Workflows

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
section: A CDI Pattern for AI-Assisted Reports
text:
A CDI Pattern for AI-Assisted Reports
A useful AI-assisted report should move from evidence to interpretation to action.
Question
  What is the report trying to answer?

Inputs
  What data, tables, plots, notes, or documents were used?

Methods
  How were the outputs produced?

Results
  What was observed?

Interpretation
  What do the results suggest?

Limitations
  What should not be overclaimed?

Recommended next steps
  What should the human team do next?
This structure helps prevent the report from becoming a loose narrative.
It also helps reviewers check whether every claim is connected to evidence.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
section: Example: Turning a Results Table into a Report Summary
text:
Example: Turning a Results Table into a Report Summary
Suppose an AI evaluation workflow creates this file:
data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv
A human should inspect the file first.
cat data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv
Then the human can ask AI for a summary using a grounded prompt.
You are helping write a short analytical summary for a CDI AI Systems guide.

Use only the results below.
Do not invent additional results.
Do not claim that the workflow is production-ready.
Separate observations, interpretation, and limitations.

Project context:
This table summarizes example AI task evaluations across criteria such as clarity, grounding, completeness, risk level, and review status.

Audience:
Learners building responsible AI workflows.

Results table:
[paste table here]

Write:
1. a short results summary,
2. a brief interpretation,
3. two limitations,
4. two recommended next steps.
This prompt is safer than asking:
Write a report about my AI system.
The better prompt gives the AI context, boundaries, evidence, and reporting structure.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
text:
Reporting from Plots and Figures
AI can help describe a figure, but the human should verify the description.
A good figure-summary prompt includes:
Figure title:
AI Evaluation Summary

Figure source:
data/reports/ai-evaluation-summary.png

Data source:
data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv

What the figure shows:
[paste your own description]

Task:
Write a concise figure caption and interpretation.
Separate what is directly visible from what is inferred.
Do not make claims beyond the figure.
This avoids treating visual interpretation as automatic truth.
The human still checks the figure, the source data, and the written claim.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
text:
AI-Assisted Reporting Checklist
Before using AI to assist with reporting, ask:

What evidence is the report based on?
Has the evidence been checked?
What is the intended audience?
What decisions might the report influence?
What claims are supported?
What claims are not supported?
Are uncertainty and limitations included?
Are sensitive details protected?
Can another person trace the report back to the inputs?
Has a human reviewed the final version?

A report that cannot be traced back to its evidence should not be treated as reliable.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
text:
Common Failure Modes
AI-assisted analysis and reporting can fail in predictable ways.








Failure mode
What happens
Prevention




Unsupported claim
AI states something not shown in the data
Require evidence-linked summaries


Overgeneralization
A small example becomes a broad conclusion
Add scope and limitation statements


Method confusion
AI mixes up tools, models, or data steps
Provide a methods summary


Citation weakness
AI invents or misuses references
Verify all references manually


Missing uncertainty
AI writes with too much confidence
Ask for limitations and caveats


Audience mismatch
Output is too technical or too vague
Define the intended audience


Privacy leakage
Sensitive information appears in outputs
Remove or mask sensitive inputs



These are not reasons to avoid AI.
They are reasons to design the workflow properly.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
section: The Role of Scripts in Reporting
text:
The Role of Scripts in Reporting
In a CDI guide, AI-assisted reporting should connect to files generated by scripts.
For example, the scaffold includes scripts that create and summarize an example AI task log.
bash scripts/bash/01-create-example-ai-task-log.sh

python scripts/python/02-evaluate-ai-output-quality.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results

python scripts/python/03-build-prompt-registry.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results

Rscript scripts/R/04-plot-evaluation-summary.R \
  data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv \
  data/reports
These commands produce structured outputs that can support reporting.
data/example/ai-task-log.csv
data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv
data/results/prompt-registry.csv
data/reports/ai-evaluation-summary.png
The key point is that AI should not be asked to report from memory.
It should report from traceable project outputs.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
text:
A Reproducible AI Reporting Workflow
A reproducible reporting workflow should make the path from input to report visible.
Project question
        ↓
Input data, notes, prompts, or task logs
        ↓
Scripted summaries and checks
        ↓
AI-assisted draft explanation
        ↓
Human review against evidence
        ↓
Final report with limitations and next steps
This pattern connects AI Systems to the broader CDI ecosystem.
The goal is not simply to write faster.
The goal is to write more clearly while preserving evidence, judgment, and accountability.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
text:
Key Takeaways
AI-assisted analysis and reporting are most useful when the evidence is already organized.
AI can help summarize, structure, explain, and revise.
It should not replace data validation, statistical reasoning, domain expertise, or final human review.
A responsible report separates results, interpretation, limitations, and next steps.
The Human-AI-Human workflow keeps the reporting process grounded: the human prepares the evidence, AI assists with drafting or explanation, and the human verifies and approves the final communication.

title: AI-Assisted Analysis and Reporting
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter moves from reporting into governance, risk, and documentation.
We will examine how AI workflows should be documented, reviewed, and managed so they remain responsible beyond a single use case.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
Why Governance Matters
AI systems are not responsible simply because they use advanced tools.
They become responsible when humans define boundaries, document decisions, review outputs, manage risks, and remain accountable for how AI-supported work is used.
This chapter introduces governance, risk, and documentation as practical parts of AI systems design.
The goal is not to create bureaucracy.
The goal is to make AI-supported work clear, reviewable, reproducible, and safe enough for the context in which it will be used.
AI tools can generate text, summarize documents, write code, classify information, draft reports, and support decisions.
That power creates value.
It also creates risk.
AI-generated work can be:
Governance is the set of practices that prevents AI from becoming an uncontrolled black box inside a workflow.
In CDI terms, governance answers five questions:
A workflow without governance may still be useful.
But it is not yet a dependable system.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
inaccurate,
incomplete,
biased,
outdated,
poorly grounded,
overconfident,
difficult to trace,
or used outside its proper context.




What is the AI system allowed to do?
What information is it allowed to use?
What outputs can it produce?
Who reviews those outputs?
Who is accountable for the final decision?

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
section: Governance Is Part of the Human–AI–Human Workflow
text:
Governance Is Part of the Human–AI–Human Workflow
The Human–AI–Human workflow remains the central pattern.
Human frames the task
        ↓
AI assists with generation, analysis, synthesis, or automation
        ↓
Human reviews, decides, documents, and acts
Governance strengthens each part of that loop.
At the first human step, governance defines the task, boundary, data source, risk level, and intended use.
At the AI step, governance controls what context is provided, what tools are used, and what outputs are expected.
At the final human step, governance requires review, documentation, approval, and accountability.
The system is not complete when AI produces an answer.
The system is complete only when the output has been reviewed and its use has been documented.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
AI Risk Is Context-Dependent
The same AI capability can be low risk in one setting and high risk in another.
For example, asking AI to rewrite a paragraph for clarity is usually low risk.
Asking AI to summarize a medical record, recommend a clinical action, review a legal contract, or evaluate a person for an opportunity is much higher risk.
Risk depends on:

the task,
the data,
the audience,
the possible harm,
the level of automation,
the review process,
and the consequences of error.

A CDI AI system should classify tasks before deciding how much review is needed.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
A Practical AI Risk Scale
A simple risk scale is useful for most applied workflows.









Risk level
Description
Example
Minimum control




Low
Errors are unlikely to cause harm
Rewriting a non-sensitive paragraph
Human review before use


Moderate
Errors may affect interpretation or communication
Summarizing project findings
Source checking and documented review


High
Errors may affect decisions, people, money, health, or compliance
Drafting policy, clinical, legal, financial, or eligibility-related content
Expert review and approval


Prohibited or restricted
Task should not be delegated to AI without formal controls
Fully automated high-stakes decisions
Do not automate without governance framework



This scale is not a legal or regulatory classification.
It is a practical design tool.
The higher the risk, the more documentation and human oversight the workflow needs.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
What Should Be Documented
AI systems become easier to trust when important choices are recorded.
A lightweight AI workflow record should include:

task name,
purpose,
intended audience,
input data or source materials,
prompt or instruction used,
model or tool used,
output generated,
known limitations,
review criteria,
reviewer,
decision made,
date,
and final status.

Documentation does not need to be complicated.
It only needs to make the workflow understandable later.
If someone asks, “How was this output produced?” the system should be able to answer.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
The AI Task Log
One of the simplest documentation tools is an AI task log.
An AI task log records what AI was used for and how the output was reviewed.
A basic task log may look like this:
task_id,task_name,risk_level,input_source,ai_role,human_review_required,review_status,final_use
AIS-001,Rewrite guide introduction,low,chapter draft,editing,true,approved,published
AIS-002,Summarize interview notes,moderate,meeting notes,summarization,true,needs revision,not used
AIS-003,Draft policy recommendation,high,internal report,drafting,true,expert review required,not finalized
This guide includes an example task log workflow.
bash scripts/bash/01-create-example-ai-task-log.sh
The task log can then be evaluated using the supporting Python script.
python scripts/python/02-evaluate-ai-output-quality.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results
The purpose of this script is not to replace human judgment.
It creates a simple summary that supports review and reporting.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
Prompt Registries as Governance Tools
A prompt registry records reusable prompts and their purpose.
This is useful when a team wants consistent AI-supported workflows.
A prompt registry should document:

prompt name,
task type,
expected input,
expected output,
review requirements,
risk level,
version,
and owner.

This guide includes a supporting prompt registry script.
python scripts/python/03-build-prompt-registry.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results
A prompt registry helps prevent hidden workflow drift.
When prompts change, the workflow changes.
When the workflow changes, the documentation should change too.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
Governance Questions Before Automation
Before any AI workflow is automated, the following questions should be answered:

Is the task clearly defined?
Is the input source known and appropriate?
Is the output reviewable?
Is the risk level documented?
Is a human reviewer assigned?
Are failure modes known?
Is there a process for stopping or correcting the workflow?
Is the final decision made by a human when needed?

Automation should not be used to avoid responsibility.
Automation should be used only when the workflow is already clear, bounded, and reviewable.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
Common Governance Failure Modes
AI workflows often fail because governance is added too late.
Common failure modes include:

treating AI output as final,
using sensitive data without clear boundaries,
failing to record prompts or sources,
automating tasks before they are understood,
not assigning human accountability,
ignoring uncertainty,
using AI outputs outside their intended context,
and publishing outputs without review.

These failures are usually not caused by one bad prompt.
They are caused by weak system design.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
section: Responsible Documentation Is a Design Habit
text:
Responsible Documentation Is a Design Habit
Documentation should not be treated as an afterthought.
It is part of the workflow.
A useful AI system leaves behind evidence of:

what was asked,
what was used,
what was generated,
what was checked,
what was changed,
what was approved,
and what was finally used.

This evidence supports transparency, learning, improvement, and accountability.
In CDI guides, documentation is not separate from analysis.
Documentation is how analysis becomes a system.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
Governance Checklist
Before using AI output in a real workflow, check the following:

The task is clearly described.
The risk level is known.
The input sources are documented.
Sensitive information has been handled appropriately.
The prompt or instruction is recorded when needed.
The AI output has been reviewed by a human.
Important claims are checked against evidence.
Limitations are stated.
The final decision-maker is clear.
The final use of the output is documented.

If several of these are missing, the workflow is not ready for operational use.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
Chapter Summary
Governance is what turns AI use into accountable AI systems.
Risk classification helps determine how much review and documentation are needed.
Documentation creates traceability.
Prompt registries and task logs make AI workflows easier to review, repeat, improve, and govern.
The Human–AI–Human workflow remains the core pattern: humans define the task, AI assists, and humans review, decide, document, and act.

title: Governance, Risk, and Documentation
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter brings the pieces together in an end-to-end case study.
We will move from isolated AI tasks into a complete workflow that includes task framing, prompt design, grounding, evaluation, review, documentation, and reporting.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Purpose of This Chapter
This chapter brings the guide together through a complete AI Systems workflow.
The goal is not to show AI as a shortcut.
The goal is to show how AI can be placed inside a responsible, human-led system.
In earlier chapters, we developed the main components of AI Systems:
This chapter connects those parts into one reproducible case study.
The central workflow remains:
This is the Human–AI–Human workflow.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
AI systems thinking
AI literacy and responsible use
problem framing
prompt and context design
data and knowledge grounding
evaluation and quality control
human-in-the-loop review
automation readiness
reporting
governance and documentation



Human frames the problem.
AI assists with generation, reasoning, analysis, or automation.
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Case Study Scenario
Imagine a small organization wants to use AI to help prepare a weekly insight brief.
The brief may summarize project updates, identify risks, suggest next steps, and produce a short communication for stakeholders.
This sounds simple.
But if handled casually, several problems can occur:

the AI may invent information
sensitive information may be exposed
unclear prompts may produce inconsistent results
outputs may be copied without review
decisions may be made without traceability
no one may know which sources were used
the workflow may become difficult to repeat

An AI Systems approach treats this as a workflow design problem, not only a prompting problem.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
The End-to-End AI Systems Workflow
The case study follows seven steps.
1. Define the task
2. Classify the risk
3. Prepare context
4. Design the prompt
5. Generate the AI-assisted output
6. Evaluate and review the output
7. Document the decision and next action
Each step creates an output that can be reviewed, reused, or improved.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Step 1: Define the Task
The human begins by defining the task clearly.
A weak task definition might be:
Use AI to summarize our work.
A stronger task definition is:
Prepare a weekly project insight brief for internal stakeholders.
The brief should summarize completed work, open issues, risks, and recommended next steps.
The output should be concise, evidence-based, and reviewed by a human before sharing.
The stronger version defines:

the purpose
the audience
the expected output
the evidence requirement
the human review requirement

This makes the workflow easier to evaluate.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Step 2: Classify the Risk
Before using AI, the task should be classified by risk.
For this case study, the weekly brief is a medium-risk task.
It is not a final medical, legal, financial, or hiring decision.
But it may influence planning, communication, and organizational priorities.
That means the output should not be accepted automatically.
Risk level: Medium
Human review required: Yes
Source grounding required: Yes
Automation allowed: Partial only
Final decision authority: Human
The purpose of risk classification is not to avoid AI.
The purpose is to decide what level of review is needed.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Step 3: Prepare Context
AI performs better when the task context is explicit.
For this case study, the human prepares a small context packet.
Project name: AI Systems Guide
Audience: CDI learners, analysts, researchers, and workflow builders
Reporting period: Current week
Primary goal: summarize progress and identify next steps
Allowed sources: project notes, task logs, chapter drafts, and confirmed outputs
Not allowed: inventing completed work, guessing private information, or making unsupported claims
This context can be stored as a reusable file.
For example:
project-context.md
A context file helps make the workflow repeatable.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Step 4: Design the Prompt
The prompt should include the task, context, constraints, and expected output.
A reusable prompt structure may look like this:
Role:
You are assisting with a weekly project insight brief.

Task:
Summarize the project progress using only the provided context.

Context:
[Paste or attach project notes, task logs, or confirmed updates.]

Rules:
- Do not invent completed work.
- Separate confirmed progress from recommended next steps.
- Flag uncertainty clearly.
- Keep the tone professional and concise.
- Produce a draft for human review, not a final decision.

Output format:
1. Progress summary
2. Key outputs
3. Risks or blockers
4. Recommended next steps
5. Items needing human confirmation
This prompt is not only a message to an AI tool.
It is part of the workflow documentation.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Step 5: Generate the AI-Assisted Output
The AI output should be treated as a draft.
The human should not ask:
Is the output polished?
The better question is:
Is the output accurate, grounded, useful, and safe to act on?
The AI-generated draft may include:

a summary of completed work
a list of outputs
unresolved issues
recommended next steps
items needing confirmation

But the draft remains provisional until reviewed.

title: End-to-End Case Study
section: Step 6: Evaluate and Review the Output
text:
Step 6: Evaluate and Review the Output
The evaluation step checks whether the output is usable.
A simple review checklist may include:
Accuracy:
Does the output match the provided evidence?

Completeness:
Does the output address the requested sections?

Grounding:
Can each important claim be traced to the context?

Clarity:
Is the output understandable to the intended audience?

Safety:
Does the output avoid exposing sensitive information or making unsupported claims?

Actionability:
Are the next steps realistic and appropriate?
The human reviewer should edit, correct, or reject the output as needed.
This is where the second human step in Human–AI–Human becomes essential.

title: End-to-End Case Study
section: Step 7: Document the Decision and Next Action
text:
Step 7: Document the Decision and Next Action
The final step is documentation.
The organization should record:

the task
the prompt or prompt version
the context sources
the model or tool used, if relevant
the reviewer
the review outcome
the final action taken

This can be captured in a task log.
Task ID: AI-BRIEF-001
Task: Weekly project insight brief
Risk level: Medium
Input sources: project notes, chapter draft status, task log
AI role: Drafting and organization
Human reviewer: Project lead
Review result: Edited and approved
Final action: Shared with internal stakeholders
Documentation makes the workflow auditable and easier to improve.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Supporting Example Workflow
This guide includes example scripts that support the case study.
Create the example task log:
bash scripts/bash/01-create-example-ai-task-log.sh
Evaluate AI output quality:
python scripts/python/02-evaluate-ai-output-quality.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results
Build a prompt registry:
python scripts/python/03-build-prompt-registry.py \
  data/example/ai-task-log.csv \
  data/results
Plot the evaluation summary:
Rscript scripts/R/04-plot-evaluation-summary.R \
  data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv \
  data/reports
These scripts are intentionally simple.
They demonstrate the system logic:
AI task log
        ↓
Evaluation summary
        ↓
Prompt registry
        ↓
Reviewable report artifact
The point is not to automate judgment.
The point is to make AI-assisted work visible, reviewable, and reusable.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Expected Outputs
After running the supporting workflow, the project should contain outputs such as:
data/example/ai-task-log.csv
data/results/ai-evaluation-summary.csv
data/results/prompt-registry.csv
data/reports/ai-evaluation-summary.png
These outputs can be used in reporting, review, and governance.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Case Study Interpretation
This case study shows that a responsible AI workflow is not built from a prompt alone.
It requires a system.
The system includes:

a clear human-defined problem
a known audience
explicit task boundaries
prepared context
grounded prompting
quality evaluation
human review
documentation
controlled automation

This is what separates AI tool use from AI Systems.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Common Failure Modes
Common failures in end-to-end AI workflows include:

starting with a vague task
skipping risk classification
using weak or missing context
asking AI to make unsupported claims
treating AI output as final
failing to document the prompt
failing to document review decisions
automating before the workflow is stable

Most failures are not model failures alone.
They are workflow design failures.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Practical Checklist
Before using AI in a repeatable workflow, ask:
Have we defined the task clearly?
Have we identified the audience?
Have we classified the risk?
Have we prepared the context?
Have we designed a reusable prompt?
Have we defined evaluation criteria?
Have we assigned human review responsibility?
Have we documented the final decision?
Have we decided what should and should not be automated?
If the answer is no, the workflow is not ready for operational use.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Key Takeaways
An end-to-end AI Systems workflow begins with human problem framing and ends with human judgment.
AI can assist with drafting, reasoning, analysis, summarization, and automation.
But responsible use requires grounding, evaluation, review, and documentation.
The Human–AI–Human workflow is not a slogan.
It is an operational design principle.

title: End-to-End Case Study
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter focuses on career and workforce readiness.
We will move from workflow design into the practical skills people need to work effectively with AI systems: AI literacy, problem framing, evaluation, documentation, communication, and responsible automation.
By the end of the next chapter, AI will not only be treated as a tool to use, but as a capability to develop across roles, teams, and organizations.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
text:
Purpose of This Chapter
This chapter connects AI Systems to career and workforce readiness.
The goal is not to encourage people to chase every new AI tool.
The goal is to help people build durable capabilities that remain useful as tools change.
AI tools will continue to evolve.
Interfaces will change.
Models will improve.
New automation patterns will appear.
But the core professional skill remains the same:
That means knowing how to frame problems, prepare context, evaluate outputs, protect sensitive information, document decisions, and communicate results clearly.
This is the professional value of AI Systems.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
text: Use AI inside a responsible human-led workflow.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
section: AI Readiness Is More Than Prompting
text:
AI Readiness Is More Than Prompting
Many people first encounter AI through prompting.
Prompting is useful.
But prompting alone is not enough for professional work.
A person may know how to ask an AI tool for an answer and still fail to build a reliable workflow.
AI readiness requires several connected abilities.
AI literacy
Problem framing
Context design
Data and knowledge grounding
Output evaluation
Human review
Documentation
Responsible automation
Communication
Governance awareness
These abilities turn AI use from isolated experimentation into a reproducible system.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
text:
The Workforce Shift
AI is changing work in three major ways.
First, routine information tasks can be accelerated.
Examples include summarizing documents, drafting first versions, organizing notes, extracting themes, preparing checklists, and creating structured outputs.
Second, analytical and communication workflows can become more interactive.
People can use AI to explore ideas, test assumptions, compare options, critique drafts, and prepare explanations for different audiences.
Third, some workflows can become partially automated.
This may include recurring reports, task classification, document review, quality checks, or workflow monitoring.
The important point is that AI does not remove the need for human judgment.
It changes where human judgment is most needed.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
section: The Human Role Becomes More Important
text:
The Human Role Becomes More Important
In weak AI workflows, people use AI as if it were an authority.
In strong AI workflows, people use AI as part of a system that remains accountable to humans.
The Human–AI–Human workflow still applies.
Human defines the problem.
AI assists with generation, reasoning, analysis, or automation.
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts.
This pattern is also a career readiness model.
Professionals who can use AI responsibly will not only ask better prompts.
They will also design better workflows.
They will know when AI is useful.
They will know when AI is risky.
They will know when human expertise, evidence, policy, or stakeholder review is required.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
text:
Core AI Systems Competencies
AI Systems readiness can be organized into practical competencies.







Competency
What it means




AI literacy
Understand what AI can and cannot do


Problem framing
Convert vague needs into clear tasks


Context design
Provide useful background, constraints, and output expectations


Grounding
Connect AI outputs to data, documents, evidence, or known project context


Evaluation
Check accuracy, completeness, relevance, and risk


Human review
Decide what requires expert review before use


Documentation
Keep records of prompts, assumptions, outputs, and decisions


Communication
Convert outputs into clear messages for real audiences


Automation judgment
Know which tasks are safe to automate and which are not


Governance awareness
Understand privacy, security, fairness, accountability, and traceability



These are transferable skills.
They apply across data science, research, healthcare, education, business, public service, and community work.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
text:
Skill Level Progression
AI Systems learning can be viewed as a progression.
Level 1: Tool user
Level 2: Responsible AI user
Level 3: Workflow designer
Level 4: AI systems builder
Level 5: AI governance-aware leader
Each level builds on the previous one.
A tool user can ask AI for help.
A responsible AI user understands privacy, limitations, and review needs.
A workflow designer can structure repeatable Human–AI–Human workflows.
An AI systems builder can connect prompts, data, scripts, reports, evaluation, and documentation.
A governance-aware leader can help teams decide how AI should be used responsibly.
The purpose of this guide is to move learners beyond Level 1.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
section: AI Skills for Analysts and Data Professionals
text:
AI Skills for Analysts and Data Professionals
For analysts and data professionals, AI readiness does not replace data skills.
It strengthens them.
AI can help with:

clarifying analytical questions
drafting data dictionaries
reviewing analysis plans
suggesting quality checks
summarizing results
explaining limitations
preparing reports
translating technical findings for nontechnical audiences

But AI should not replace the analyst’s responsibility to inspect data, validate results, understand methods, and communicate uncertainty.
A strong AI-assisted analysis workflow should still produce defensible outputs.
input data
analysis logic
quality checks
results table
interpretation
review notes
final report
The analyst remains responsible for the claim.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
section: AI Skills for Researchers and Educators
text:
AI Skills for Researchers and Educators
For researchers and educators, AI can support reading, synthesis, drafting, teaching, and feedback.
Useful applications include:

summarizing literature notes
comparing concepts
generating teaching examples
preparing discussion questions
drafting outlines
reviewing clarity
converting technical ideas into learner-friendly language

But responsible use requires care.
Research and education workflows must protect intellectual integrity.
AI should not fabricate sources.
AI should not replace critical reading.
AI should not be used to hide weak understanding.
In a strong workflow, AI supports thinking, but humans remain responsible for learning, interpretation, and final communication.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
section: AI Skills for Managers and Teams
text:
AI Skills for Managers and Teams
For managers and teams, the main AI skill is not only using AI personally.
It is creating conditions where AI can be used safely and productively.
Teams need shared expectations.
These may include:

which AI tools are allowed
what information must not be entered into AI tools
which tasks require review
how AI-assisted outputs should be documented
when AI-generated content must be labeled or disclosed
how errors should be reported
how workflows should be improved over time

Without shared expectations, AI use becomes inconsistent and risky.
With shared expectations, AI can improve productivity while preserving accountability.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
section: Building a Personal AI Systems Portfolio
text:
Building a Personal AI Systems Portfolio
A useful way to demonstrate AI readiness is to build a small portfolio of responsible workflows.
This does not need to be complicated.
A portfolio can include examples such as:

a prompt registry
an AI task log
a before-and-after workflow improvement
a grounded summary workflow
an evaluation checklist
a report generated from reviewed outputs
a governance note for a team use case
a documented Human–AI–Human workflow

The goal is to show that you can use AI responsibly and systematically.
A strong portfolio does not only show outputs.
It shows process.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
text:
Example Portfolio Structure
A simple AI Systems portfolio could use the following structure.
ai-systems-portfolio/
├── README.md
├── examples/
│   ├── weekly-brief-workflow.md
│   ├── grounded-summary-workflow.md
│   └── ai-assisted-reporting-workflow.md
├── prompts/
│   └── prompt-registry.csv
├── evaluation/
│   └── ai-output-review-checklist.md
├── governance/
│   └── responsible-use-note.md
└── reports/
    └── example-reviewed-output.md
This type of portfolio communicates that the person understands AI as a system, not just as a chat interface.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
text:
Workforce Readiness Checklist
Before claiming AI readiness, a learner or team should be able to answer these questions.
Can I define an AI task clearly?
Can I identify whether the task is low, medium, or high risk?
Can I decide what context the AI needs?
Can I avoid entering sensitive or inappropriate information?
Can I evaluate the output before using it?
Can I identify unsupported claims?
Can I document prompts, outputs, assumptions, and review decisions?
Can I decide when human expert review is required?
Can I explain the workflow to someone else?
Can I improve the workflow after observing errors?
These questions are simple, but they are powerful.
They move AI use from casual experimentation toward responsible practice.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
section: Common Career Mistakes in AI Adoption
text:
Common Career Mistakes in AI Adoption
Several mistakes can slow professional growth with AI.







Mistake
Better practice




Learning only tools
Learn workflows and evaluation habits


Copying AI output directly
Review, revise, and document before use


Ignoring risk
Classify tasks before using AI


Treating AI as always correct
Require evidence and human judgment


Automating too early
Stabilize the workflow first


Hiding AI use where disclosure is needed
Follow organizational and ethical expectations


Using sensitive data casually
Protect privacy and confidentiality


Producing outputs without records
Maintain task logs and prompt registries



These mistakes are not only technical.
They are professional practice issues.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
section: From AI User to AI Systems Designer
text:
From AI User to AI Systems Designer
The professional shift can be summarized this way.
AI user:
I ask AI to help me complete a task.

AI systems designer:
I design a workflow where AI assists, humans remain accountable, and outputs can be reviewed, documented, and improved.
This distinction is central to the CDI approach.
AI Systems is not about using AI everywhere.
It is about knowing how to place AI where it adds value and where human oversight remains clear.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
text:
Practical Exercise
Choose one recurring task from your own work or learning environment.
Examples:

writing a weekly update
summarizing meeting notes
preparing a learning plan
reviewing a draft report
organizing project risks
translating technical results into plain language

Then complete this template.
Task name:
Purpose:
Audience:
Current workflow:
Where AI could assist:
Required context:
Potential risks:
Human review checkpoint:
Expected output:
Documentation needed:
Decision after review:
This exercise turns AI readiness into something practical.
It begins with one real workflow.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
text:
Chapter Summary
AI workforce readiness is not only about knowing which AI tools exist.
It is about building durable skills for responsible AI use.
The most important skills include problem framing, context design, grounding, evaluation, documentation, communication, and governance awareness.
Professionals who develop these skills can move beyond isolated AI use and begin designing responsible, reproducible, human-led AI workflows.
The Human–AI–Human workflow remains the foundation.
Human defines the problem.
AI assists.
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts.
That is the career value of AI Systems.

title: Career and Workforce Readiness
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter provides appendix materials and reusable reference templates.
These resources support the full AI Systems workflow, including problem framing, prompt design, evaluation, documentation, governance, and Human–AI–Human review.

title: Appendix
text:
Purpose of This Appendix
This appendix collects reusable structures from the guide.
The main chapters explain the reasoning behind AI Systems.
This appendix provides practical templates that can be copied into projects, team workflows, training programs, or documentation.
The goal is not to make AI use complicated.
The goal is to make AI use clear, reviewable, reproducible, and human-led.

title: Appendix
text:
Core CDI AI Systems Pattern
The central workflow in this guide is the Human–AI–Human pattern.
Human defines the problem.
AI assists with generation, reasoning, analysis, or automation.
Human evaluates, decides, documents, and acts.
This pattern applies whether the task is simple or advanced.
It applies to writing, analysis, coding, summarization, reporting, teaching, planning, automation, and decision support.
AI Systems are not built by asking AI to do everything.
They are built by placing AI inside a responsible human workflow.

title: Appendix
text:
AI Task Framing Template
Use this template before asking AI to perform meaningful work.
AI Task Framing Template

1. Task name:

2. Human goal:

3. Intended audience:

4. Context the AI should use:

5. Context the AI should avoid:

6. Input materials:

7. Expected output format:

8. Quality criteria:

9. Risk level:
   Low / Medium / High

10. Human review required before use:
   Yes / No

11. Decision or action after review:
A task is usually not ready for AI if the goal, audience, context, and review criteria are unclear.

title: Appendix
text:
AI Task Risk Levels
Not every AI task carries the same risk.
A quick risk classification helps decide how much review is needed.









Risk level
Description
Examples
Review expectation




Low
Low-impact support task
Rewording, brainstorming, formatting, first-pass summaries
Light human review


Medium
Work that may influence interpretation, planning, communication, or workflow design
Drafting reports, summarizing evidence, generating code, preparing teaching materials
Careful human review


High
Work that may influence health, legal, financial, safety, hiring, disciplinary, or other consequential decisions
Clinical recommendations, legal claims, financial advice, high-stakes automation
Expert review, documentation, and governance



AI output should not be treated as final in medium- or high-risk tasks.

title: Appendix
text:
Prompt Design Template
A reusable prompt should make the task, context, constraints, and output format explicit.
Prompt Design Template

Role:
You are helping with...

Task:
Please produce...

Context:
Use the following information...

Constraints:
Do not assume...
Do not invent...
Flag uncertainty where needed...

Output format:
Return the result as...

Quality criteria:
The output should be clear, accurate, grounded, concise, and reviewable.

Human review:
Highlight anything that requires human checking before use.
Good prompts do not remove the need for judgment.
They make judgment easier.

title: Appendix
text:
Context Design Checklist
Before sending context into an AI workflow, check whether the context is appropriate.
Context Design Checklist

[ ] The task goal is clear.
[ ] The audience is clear.
[ ] The relevant source material is included.
[ ] Irrelevant material has been removed.
[ ] Sensitive information has been excluded or minimized.
[ ] The AI is told what to prioritize.
[ ] The AI is told what not to assume.
[ ] The expected output format is clear.
[ ] The review criteria are clear.
Poor context often creates poor AI output, even when the prompt sounds polished.

title: Appendix
text:
Grounding Checklist
Use this checklist when AI output needs to be connected to data, documents, evidence, or project materials.
Grounding Checklist

[ ] What source material supports the output?
[ ] Is the source material current enough for the task?
[ ] Are important assumptions stated?
[ ] Are claims traceable to evidence?
[ ] Are uncertain points flagged?
[ ] Are interpretations separated from facts?
[ ] Are citations or references needed?
[ ] Has a human checked the final output against the source material?
Grounding is especially important for analysis, reporting, teaching, governance, and decision-support workflows.

title: Appendix
text:
AI Output Evaluation Rubric
Use this rubric to review AI-generated outputs.







Criterion
Review question




Relevance
Does the output answer the actual task?


Accuracy
Are the claims correct and supported?


Grounding
Is the output connected to the provided context or evidence?


Completeness
Are important parts missing?


Clarity
Is the output understandable to the intended audience?


Safety
Could the output create harm if used as written?


Actionability
Is the next step clear?


Traceability
Can a reviewer understand how the output was produced and checked?



A useful AI output is not only fluent.
It must be fit for purpose.

title: Appendix
text:
Human Review Checklist
Use this before sharing, publishing, acting on, or automating AI-assisted work.
Human Review Checklist

[ ] I understand the task the AI was asked to perform.
[ ] I reviewed the output for accuracy.
[ ] I checked whether the output is grounded in appropriate context.
[ ] I identified unsupported claims.
[ ] I corrected misleading or unclear language.
[ ] I checked for missing risks or limitations.
[ ] I verified any important numbers, names, references, or claims.
[ ] I decided whether the output is ready to use.
[ ] I documented any important changes or concerns.
Human review is not a formality.
It is part of the system.

title: Appendix
text:
Prompt Registry Template
A prompt registry helps teams reuse and improve prompts over time.
Prompt Registry Template

prompt_id:

prompt_name:

workflow_stage:

intended_user:

input_requirements:

prompt_text:

expected_output:

quality_criteria:

risk_level:

reviewer:

version:

last_updated:

notes:
A prompt registry turns informal AI use into a documented workflow asset.

title: Appendix
text:
AI Task Log Template
An AI task log records what happened in an AI-supported workflow.
AI Task Log Template

task_id:

date:

user_or_team:

task_name:

purpose:

tool_used:

input_context:

output_summary:

risk_level:

human_review_status:

issues_found:

final_decision:

follow_up_action:
Task logs are especially useful when AI output supports reports, analysis, automation, or decisions.

title: Appendix
text:
Automation Readiness Checklist
A workflow should not be automated simply because it can be automated.
Use this checklist before turning an AI task into a repeatable or agentic workflow.
Automation Readiness Checklist

[ ] The task is repeated often enough to justify automation.
[ ] The inputs are predictable.
[ ] The expected outputs are clear.
[ ] The quality criteria are defined.
[ ] Failure modes are known.
[ ] Human review checkpoints are included.
[ ] Sensitive information is handled safely.
[ ] Logs or records are produced.
[ ] There is a clear owner for maintaining the workflow.
[ ] The workflow can be stopped, corrected, or rolled back.
Automation without review can scale mistakes.
Responsible automation scales reliable practice.

title: Appendix
text:
Governance Documentation Template
AI governance does not need to begin with heavy bureaucracy.
It can begin with clear documentation.
AI Governance Documentation Template

Workflow name:

Workflow owner:

Purpose:

Users:

AI tools used:

Data or context used:

Risk level:

Human review checkpoint:

Known limitations:

Privacy considerations:

Output use restrictions:

Approval or review process:

Update schedule:

Incident or issue process:
Governance should make responsible AI use easier, not harder.

text:
Example Project Structure
A small AI Systems project may use a structure like this:
ai-systems-project/
├── README.md
├── prompts/
│   ├── prompt-registry.csv
│   └── templates/
├── data/

title: Appendix
text:
CDI AI Systems Learning Path
The guide follows this learning sequence:
AI systems thinking
AI literacy and responsible use
Problem framing
Prompt and context design
Data and knowledge grounding
Evaluation and quality control
Human-in-the-loop workflows
Automation and agentic workflows
AI-assisted analysis and reporting
Governance, risk, and documentation
End-to-end case study
Career and workforce readiness
The sequence moves from thinking to practice, then from practice to systems.

title: Appendix
text:
Glossary
AI system
A human-led workflow that uses AI tools, context, review, documentation, and governance to support a defined task or outcome.
Human–AI–Human workflow
A workflow in which humans frame the problem, AI assists, and humans evaluate, decide, document, and act.
Prompt
An instruction or set of instructions given to an AI system.
Context
The background information, data, examples, constraints, or documents provided to guide AI output.
Grounding
Connecting AI output to source material, data, evidence, or documented context.
Evaluation
The process of checking whether AI output is accurate, useful, safe, and fit for purpose.
Human-in-the-loop
A workflow design where humans review, approve, correct, or decide at important points.
Automation
A repeatable workflow where some steps are executed automatically.
Agentic workflow
A workflow where AI is given more autonomy to plan or execute steps within defined boundaries.
Governance
The rules, roles, documentation, review processes, and accountability structures used to manage AI responsibly.

title: Appendix
text:
Looking Ahead
The references chapter lists selected sources that support responsible AI use, human-centered AI systems, AI evaluation, and governance.
Those references are not the end of the learning path.
They provide a foundation for continuing to build AI systems that are useful, responsible, and human-led.

title: References

Suggested Citation Use
Use references where a chapter makes claims about:

governance practices
transparency and documentation
human oversight
evaluation methods

Avoid over-citing basic workflow instructions. Cite sources where they support concepts, principles, standards, or claims that readers may want to verify.
